(Hetsu Shrine)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=18}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukuoka|lat_deg=33|lat_min=53|lat_sec=16.05|lon_deg=130|lon_min=31|lon_sec=32.05|position=top|label_size=90|label='''Orihata Shrine'''|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}}}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}{{small|Mount Sayagata in the back. }}]] '''Orihata Shrine'' (Orihata Jinja) is located in {{jalink|Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258}}{{jalink|Munakata City|Q819603}}{{jalink|Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|Kanezaki}}. {{jalink|Shikinai Shrine}}, {{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964}}, and {{jalink|Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|old shrine rank}} is {{jalink|Prefectural shrine}}. Another name is "Orihataguu". Locals also call him ``Shikihan-sama''. === Enshrined God === The following seven deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Kamigun Munakata No. 1|2011|p=8}}. :* Minister Takeuchi (Takeuchi no Oomi) - {{jalink|Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668}} :* {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Three Gods|Q1114013|Sumiyoshi Okami}} (Sumiyoshi no Okami) :* Shiga Okami :* {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}} (Amaterasu no Okami) :* Munakata no Okami :* Kashii no Okami :* {{jalink|Hachimankami|Q261637|Hachiman Okami}} (Hachiman no Okami) :* {{jalink|Maneko Iki||Maneko Iki}}In "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}, which was established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), there is one enshrined deity. At the time of ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established during the {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} years ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}-{{jalink|1449|Q6446}}), it is written that Minister Takeuchi is the enshrined deity, and {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine )|2000}}, {{jalink|Early Modern Period|Q5308718}}'s ``{{jalink|Chikuzen Kunitsoku Fudoki|Q108571667}}'' states that the three deities enshrined here are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}. Currently, the eight deities listed above are enshrined, and the three main deities are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine History Book, Orihata Shrine|2025}}.During the {{jalink|Shinbutsu syncretization|Q2279088}} period, {{jalink|Honjibutsu}} was considered to be {{jalink|Nyoirin Kannon|Q11446712}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. {{jalink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}} Formerly {{jalink|Jinguji|Q1074971}} {{jalink|Chinkokuji|Q11651157}}, the original Buddhist statues of the five Munakata shrines (Fukuoka Prefecture-designated cultural properties) including Orihata Shrine have been preserved for generations. {{PDFlink|[http://city.munakata.lg.jp/w009/050/140/0010/130/13-14shinaisiteibunkazai-shuusei.pdf Cultural properties in the city]}} (Munakata City homepage). . === About the enshrined deity === The theory that the enshrined deity of Orihata Shrine is Takeuchi Sukune (Minister Takeuchi) dates back to ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established in the middle of the {{jalink|15th century|Q7018}}, and the same Engi records the founding tradition described below ({{jalink|#Sōken||Sōken-bushi}}). On the other hand, the precincts of Orihata Shrine are located at Cape Kaneno, a strategic point that juts out into the sea, and the entire area is also identified as ``Kaifu-go'' in ``{{jalink|Japanese name Ruijusho|Q2702542|Japanese namebook}}'', indicating that Kaifu existed there. Because of its location in the region, there is a theory that it was originally a sea god worshiped by the Kaijin tribe. As mentioned below ({{jalink|#tradition||tradition section}}), "{{jalink|Manyoshu|Q834321}}" When passing Cape Kaneno, there is a shrine called "Shiga no Kojin" ({{jalink|Shigaumi Shrine|Q11491171}}Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Aduji|Q3377806} }There is also a theory that the Azumi clan (Adu tribe), a member of the Kaijin tribe, resided in the area and worshiped the Shiga god, as there is a song that says they pray to the god Hosai.{{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}Also, "{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}" {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} era"Nihon Shoki" Article dated February 1, 1997 of Emperor Ojin, article of February 41, 41st year of Emperor Ojin. In , the Emperor ordered {{jalink|Achi Messenger|Q11657596}} and {{jalink|Tuka Messenger|Q24863564}} to go to Wu (Kangnan, China) and obtain sewing women, but when the Achi Messengers returned home, they were told that they had ``Chunkagata Okami'' (= There is a legend that he offered his older brother among the four female factory workers to the god Munakata Okami) at the request of the god Munakata Okami. , there is also a theory that connects this tradition with Orihata Shrine. Among these theories, it is pointed out that the weavers who weave clothes to offer to the gods may have been deified.{{Sfn|Munakata City History In General History Volume 2 | 1999 | pp=31-33}}, it is pointed out that Takeuchi Sukune may have been chosen as the enshrined deity as the common ancestor of the imported clans that were responsible for weaving {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods) | 2000}}.Of the eight deities enshrined, Maneko Iki is said to be the founder of the {{jalink|Iki clan|Q11429628}}, the former chief priest of Orihata Shrine {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. In addition to the existence of the Iki clan, the "Otenaga" that appears in the "Munakata Daibosatsu Engi" is {{jalink|Iki Island|Q494914}}'s Shikinai Shrine {{jalink|Amante Nagao Shrine|Q1 1442606}}・Since it is also associated with Amante Nagahime Shrine, there is a theory that suggests a relationship between the rituals at Orihata Shrine and Iki Island.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. In addition, there are different theories about the deity enshrined, such as the theory that it is Oomikutsu no Kami, and the theory that it is Kureori or Anaori.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}. === History === [[File:Kanezaki Port 01.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Distant view of Cape Kanezaki}}{{small|View from Kanezaki fishing port. Orihata Shrine is located halfway up Mt. Sayagata on the far left of the image. }}]] === Founding === Regarding its founding, according to "Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi" (established in the mid-15th century), during the {{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803}} {{jalink|Conquest of the Three Koreas|Q11249435}}, the Minister of Munakata ({{jal ink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}}) fought with two red and white flags woven by Minister Takeuchi ({{jalink|Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668}}) attached to a flagpole called ``Otenaga'', and in the end, they were used as ``Soku Mishima (= It was built on {{jalink|Okinoshima|Q7082047}}). At the time of Minister Takeuchi's funeral, the spirit was enshrined on the seashore to protect the sea route from invading pirates, and it was named ``Orihata (Orihata)'' because Minister Takeuchi wove the flag.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. The actual establishment is not known, but as mentioned above, there are theories that suggest a relationship with the Kaijin tribe, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Kadokawa)|1988}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}, "Nihon Shoki" and the legend of the seamstress of the Munakata deity.{{Sfn|Munakata City History General History Volume 2|1999|pp=31-33}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Outline === In {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushi}}, {{jalink|Kaxiang|Q1373157}}3 years ({{jalink|850 years|Q30364}}) In addition to the article that "Orihatakami" is given the rank of Jigoinoge, {{jalink|Shinkai|Q11591025}}, {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} 3rd year ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) Junior Fifth Rank, {{jalink|Genkei|Q747451}}1st year ({{jalink|877|Q23905}}){{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.{{jalink|extension (era name)|Q1327274|extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927 years|Q30470}}) {{jali nk|Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{jalink|Chikuzen Province|Q1047664}}{{jalink|Munakata District|Q6935606}} has ``Orihata Shrine'' It is listed as {{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964}} along with Munakata Shrine 3 (currently Munakata Taisha) (Munakata County The only Shikinai shrines are Munakata Shrine and Orihata Shrine) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Among the place names that appear in "{{jalink|Wamyo Ruijusho|Q2702542|Wamyo Sho}}" from the middle of the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} According to one theory, this area is located in Kaifu-go, Munakata District. It is presumed that around this Heian period, Orihata Shrine became a shrine under the jurisdiction of Munakata Shrine.According to the Dajokan Code draft of {{jalink|Bun'ei|Q1010103}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1265|Q5516}}), at that time the {{jali nk|Chinkokuji|Q11651157}} {{jalink|Honjibutsu}} of ``Kanezaki Orihata Myojin'' was enshrined {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Also, in the {{jalink|Kishōmon|Q11636131}} of Bun'ei 9 ({{jalink|1272|Q5524}}), ``Orihata Daimyojin'' appears {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Established in {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}-{{jalink|1449|Q6446}}) In ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' the five gods of Munakata, plus Orihata Daimyojin and Kōgi Gongen, are collectively called the ``Munakata Five Shrines'' {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), {{j alink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} Ranked as {{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978 }}, in Meiji 15 ({{jalink|1882|Q7812}}), {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}, {{jalink|Showa|Q327 In 567}}3 ({{jalink|1928|Q19715}}), it was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978}}. In the 10th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1877|Q7773}}), Munakata Shrine (currently Munakata Taisha Shrine) reported it to the government as a separate shrine along with Matohara Shrine, Kodaiji Shrine, Miyajidake Shrine, and others. === God level === * July 29, 3rd year of {{jalink|Kasho|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|850 years|Q30364}}), Jugoinoge ("{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Emperor Annals|Q1052905}}") "Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals" July 29th Kasho 3rd year (850) article (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} January 27, 3rd year ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper junior fifth rank ("{{jalink|Japan Sandai Jiroki|Q1052981}}") "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated 27th New Year, Jogan 1st year (859) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * December 15th, {{jalink|Genkei|Q747451}} 1st year ({{jalink|877|Q23905}}), from Jugoinoge {{sub|Mama}} to Shogoikege (``Nihon Sandai Jirroku'') "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" December 15th, Genkei 1st year (877) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The name is written as "Orihatakami." === Shinto priest === The rituals at Orihata Shrine are said to have been carried out by {{jalink|Iki clan|Q11429628}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. According to legend, this Iki clan is said to be a descendant of the enshrined deity Maneko Iki, and when the Heike clan fell, they changed their surname from "Iki" to "Irie," but {{jalink|Bunkyu|Q7 It is said that it was returned to "Iki" in 30266}} ({{jalink|1863|Q7701}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. After that, the Iki family retired from the position of chief priest of Orihata Shrine in {{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}18 ({{jalink|1943|Q18623}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. === Precincts === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), shaden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Shrine building}}{{small|The main shrine is on the left, and the worship hall is on the right. }}]]The precincts are located halfway up Mt. Sayakata, at the tip of Cape Kaneno, which separates {{jalink|Genkai Sea|Q498047}} and {{jalink|Hibikinada|Q11288861}}. A temperate forest has formed within the grounds of Orihata Shrine and on Mt. Sayakata, including more than 10 giant trees of {{jalink|Inumaki|Q165063}}, {{jalink|Chii|Q1065400}}, {{jalink|Hamanabiwa|Q1258941} 4}}・{{jalink|Yabunikai|Q161335}}・{{jalink|Sangoju|Q12600081}}・{{jalink|Onore}}・{{jalink|Shirodamo|Q5792530}}・{{jalink|Tabnoki |Q5792073|Tab}}・{{jalink|Tobera|Q161375}}・{{jalink|Yatsude|Q203569}}・{{jalink|Tsubaki|Q160121}}・{{jalink|Yuzuriha|Q846968}}・{{ jalink|Yamamomo|Q311617}}・{{jalink|Sanekazura|Q1441971}}・{{jalink|Fouto Kazura|Q5924111}}・{{jalink|Sakaki|Q775470}}・{{jalink|Aoki (Plants) More than 20 species grow here, including |Q163114|Aoki}}, {{jalink|Masaki|Q161383}}, {{jalink|Kijoorchid|Q5367121|Kijoorchid}}, and {{jalink|Mukunoki|Q4779482}}. This natural forest has been designated as a natural monument of Fukuoka Prefecture as the ``Orihata Shrine Inumaki Natural Forest'' (Munahaku Electronic Museum). .According to ancient documents, the current main shrine was built in {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}8 ({{jalink|1695|Q7696}}), and the worship hall was built in Genroku 16 ({{jalink|1703|Q6822}}). Several other companies are also located within the precincts. Also within the precincts are Kutsuzuka, where the enshrined deity, Minister Takeuchi, buried the shoe left after ascending to heaven, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}, and a huge stone related to the legend of the sinking bell, which will be described later. File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-rin.jpg|Precinct forest (Fukuoka Prefecture designated natural monument) File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), Kutsu-zuka.jpg|Kutsuzuka File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-1.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-2.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Cultural assets === === Fukuoka Prefecture designated cultural property === * Natural monument ** Inumaki natural forest at Orihata Shrine - Designated on August 13, 1955Explanation board within the precincts. . === Tradition === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), kyoseki.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Giant stone of the legend of the sunken bell}}]] There is a legend that a foreign bell is sunk in the sea at Cape Kanenomisaki, where Orihata Shrine is located. The place name ``Cape Kaneno'' comes from this, and similar legends of sinking bells are known all over the country, but this area and Kanegasaki in {{jalink|Fukui Prefecture|Q133879}} are especially famous . However, what was thought to be a bell on the sea floor at Cape Kaneno was brought back to land in {{jalink|Taisho|Q329174}}8 ({{jalink|1919|Q2157}}), but what was pulled up at that time was not a bell but a megalith. This megalith is currently enshrined on the approach to Orihata Shrine. Additionally, the area between Cape Kaneno and {{jalink|Jijima|Q17992764}} on the opposite shore is a reef area, and it is known that it has been a key point and difficult point for maritime transportation since ancient times. "{{jalink|Manyoushu|Q834321}}" Manyoushu volume 7 number 1230 ([http://infux03.inf.edu.yamaguchi-u.ac.jp/~manyou/ver2_2/manyou_kekka2.php?kekka=07/1230 07/1230] reference). So,{{Cquote|Chihayaburu Even if I don't pass the golden cape, I won't forget Shiga's Emperor (Sumekami)|20px||"{{jalink|Man'yoshu|Q834321}}" Volume 7 No. 1230"New Edition of Japanese Classical Literature Complete Works 7 Man'yoshu (2)” Shogakukan, 2004 ({{jalink|Japan Knowledge|Q11309996}} edition), pp. 226-227. }} There is a song about praying to the god of the sea when passing Cape Kaneno (Kin no Misaki) {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Based on this song, there is a theory that Cape Kanenosaki was once inhabited by the Azum tribe, who worshiped the god Shiga. {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{jalink|Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258}}{{jalink|Munakata City|Q819603}}{{jalink|Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 '''Access''' * {{jalink|Nishitetsu Bus|Q11629843}}/Munakata City Community Bus get off at "Kyodomari" bus stop (short walk after getting off) - There are only a few buses. '''Surroundings''' * Kanezaki (Kamihachi) Shell Midden - A historic site designated by Munakata City. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Original text''' {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References/Sites === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board '''Literature''' * Published by Munakata Taisha ** {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[http://www.munakata-taisha.or.jp/infomation/singun01.pdf "Kamigun Munakata No. 1"]}} 2011, p. 8|ref={{Harvid|Kamigun Munakata No. 1|2011}}}}. ** {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[http://www.munakata-taisha.or.jp/infomation/singun04.pdf "Shingun Munakata No. 4"]}} 2013, pp. 6-7|ref={{Harvid|Shingun Munakata No. 4|2013}}}}. * Issued by local government ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=|title=Munakata City History General History Volume 2|publisher=Munakata City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Munakata City History General History Volume 2|1999}}}} * Encyclopedia ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Orihata Shrine|title={{jalink|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|Q4093011}} 40 Fukuoka Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765}}|isbn=978-4040014005|ref={{Harvid|Orihata Shrine (Kadokawa)|1988}}}}** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=2004|chapter=|title={{jalink|Japanese historical place name system|Q17213509}} 41 Place names of Fukuoka prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490411|ref=}} *** {{Wikicite|reference="Orihata Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Kanasaki/Kanezaki"|ref={{Harvid|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}}}. * Others ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Orihata Shrine|title={{jalink|Prefecture Gosha Meiji Shrine Materials|Q11486103}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine 24|publisher={{jalink|Kogakukan University|Q1797584}}Publishing Department|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}}} ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Kenichi torii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Orihata-jinja}} [[JA:織幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11608231}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Orihata Shrine |Image = [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), haiden.jpg|270px]]
House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258}}{{jalink|Munakata City|Q819603}}{{jalink|Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|33|53|16.05|N|130|31|32.05|E|region:JP-39_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Orihata Shrine}} |Enshrined God = {{jalink|Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|Minister Takeuchi}}
{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Three Gods|Q1114013|Sumiyoshi Okami}}
Shiga Okami
{{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}}
Munakata Okami
{{jalink|Kashiikami||Kashii Okami}}
{{jalink|Hachimankami|Q261637|Hachiman Okami}}
{{jalink|Maneko Iki||Maneko Iki}} |Divinity = |Company status = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|Meishin University}})
Former {{jalink|Prefectural shrine}} |Founded = Emperor Richū year |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Alternative name = Orihata Shrine |Fudasho, etc. = Munakata Gosha|Regular Festival = {{jalink|April 16th|Q2519}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map+|Japan Fukuoka|width=200|float=center|caption=|places= {{Location map~|Japan Fukuoka|lat_deg=33|lat_min=49|lat_sec=52.37|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=51.21|position=bottom|label_size=85|label={{jalink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}}
(Hetsu Shrine)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=18}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukuoka|lat_deg=33|lat_min=53|lat_sec=16.05|lon_deg=130|lon_min=31|lon_sec=32.05|position=top|label_size=90|label='''Orihata Shrine'''|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}}}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}{{small|Mount Sayagata in the back. }}]] '''Orihata Shrine'' (Orihata Jinja) is located in {{jalink|Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258}}{{jalink|Munakata City|Q819603}}{{jalink|Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|Kanezaki}}. {{jalink|Shikinai Shrine}}, {{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964}}, and {{jalink|Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|old shrine rank}} is {{jalink|Prefectural shrine}}. Another name is "Orihataguu". Locals also call him ``Shikihan-sama''. === Enshrined God === The following seven deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Kamigun Munakata No. 1|2011|p=8}}. :* Minister Takeuchi (Takeuchi no Oomi) - {{jalink|Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668}} :* {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Three Gods|Q1114013|Sumiyoshi Okami}} (Sumiyoshi no Okami) :* Shiga Okami :* {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}} (Amaterasu no Okami) :* Munakata no Okami :* Kashii no Okami :* {{jalink|Hachimankami|Q261637|Hachiman Okami}} (Hachiman no Okami) :* {{jalink|Maneko Iki||Maneko Iki}}In "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}, which was established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), there is one enshrined deity. At the time of ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established during the {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} years ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}-{{jalink|1449|Q6446}}), it is written that Minister Takeuchi is the enshrined deity, and {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine )|2000}}, {{jalink|Early Modern Period|Q5308718}}'s ``{{jalink|Chikuzen Kunitsoku Fudoki|Q108571667}}'' states that the three deities enshrined here are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}. Currently, the eight deities listed above are enshrined, and the three main deities are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine History Book, Orihata Shrine|2025}}.During the {{jalink|Shinbutsu syncretization|Q2279088}} period, {{jalink|Honjibutsu}} was considered to be {{jalink|Nyoirin Kannon|Q11446712}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. {{jalink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}} Formerly {{jalink|Jinguji|Q1074971}} {{jalink|Chinkokuji|Q11651157}}, the original Buddhist statues of the five Munakata shrines (Fukuoka Prefecture-designated cultural properties) including Orihata Shrine have been preserved for generations. {{PDFlink|[http://city.munakata.lg.jp/w009/050/140/0010/130/13-14shinaisiteibunkazai-shuusei.pdf Cultural properties in the city]}} (Munakata City homepage). . === About the enshrined deity === The theory that the enshrined deity of Orihata Shrine is Takeuchi Sukune (Minister Takeuchi) dates back to ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established in the middle of the {{jalink|15th century|Q7018}}, and the same Engi records the founding tradition described below ({{jalink|#Sōken||Sōken-bushi}}). On the other hand, the precincts of Orihata Shrine are located at Cape Kaneno, a strategic point that juts out into the sea, and the entire area is also identified as ``Kaifu-go'' in ``{{jalink|Japanese name Ruijusho|Q2702542|Japanese namebook}}'', indicating that Kaifu existed there. Because of its location in the region, there is a theory that it was originally a sea god worshiped by the Kaijin tribe. As mentioned below ({{jalink|#tradition||tradition section}}), "{{jalink|Manyoshu|Q834321}}" When passing Cape Kaneno, there is a shrine called "Shiga no Kojin" ({{jalink|Shigaumi Shrine|Q11491171}}Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Aduji|Q3377806} }There is also a theory that the Azumi clan (Adu tribe), a member of the Kaijin tribe, resided in the area and worshiped the Shiga god, as there is a song that says they pray to the god Hosai.{{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}Also, "{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}" {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} era"Nihon Shoki" Article dated February 1, 1997 of Emperor Ojin, article of February 41, 41st year of Emperor Ojin. In , the Emperor ordered {{jalink|Achi Messenger|Q11657596}} and {{jalink|Tuka Messenger|Q24863564}} to go to Wu (Kangnan, China) and obtain sewing women, but when the Achi Messengers returned home, they were told that they had ``Chunkagata Okami'' (= There is a legend that he offered his older brother among the four female factory workers to the god Munakata Okami) at the request of the god Munakata Okami. , there is also a theory that connects this tradition with Orihata Shrine. Among these theories, it is pointed out that the weavers who weave clothes to offer to the gods may have been deified.{{Sfn|Munakata City History In General History Volume 2 | 1999 | pp=31-33}}, it is pointed out that Takeuchi Sukune may have been chosen as the enshrined deity as the common ancestor of the imported clans that were responsible for weaving {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods) | 2000}}.Of the eight deities enshrined, Maneko Iki is said to be the founder of the {{jalink|Iki clan|Q11429628}}, the former chief priest of Orihata Shrine {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. In addition to the existence of the Iki clan, the "Otenaga" that appears in the "Munakata Daibosatsu Engi" is {{jalink|Iki Island|Q494914}}'s Shikinai Shrine {{jalink|Amante Nagao Shrine|Q1 1442606}}・Since it is also associated with Amante Nagahime Shrine, there is a theory that suggests a relationship between the rituals at Orihata Shrine and Iki Island.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. In addition, there are different theories about the deity enshrined, such as the theory that it is Oomikutsu no Kami, and the theory that it is Kureori or Anaori.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}. === History === [[File:Kanezaki Port 01.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Distant view of Cape Kanezaki}}{{small|View from Kanezaki fishing port. Orihata Shrine is located halfway up Mt. Sayagata on the far left of the image. }}]] === Founding === Regarding its founding, according to "Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi" (established in the mid-15th century), during the {{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803}} {{jalink|Conquest of the Three Koreas|Q11249435}}, the Minister of Munakata ({{jal ink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}}) fought with two red and white flags woven by Minister Takeuchi ({{jalink|Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668}}) attached to a flagpole called ``Otenaga'', and in the end, they were used as ``Soku Mishima (= It was built on {{jalink|Okinoshima|Q7082047}}). At the time of Minister Takeuchi's funeral, the spirit was enshrined on the seashore to protect the sea route from invading pirates, and it was named ``Orihata (Orihata)'' because Minister Takeuchi wove the flag.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. The actual establishment is not known, but as mentioned above, there are theories that suggest a relationship with the Kaijin tribe, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Kadokawa)|1988}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}, "Nihon Shoki" and the legend of the seamstress of the Munakata deity.{{Sfn|Munakata City History General History Volume 2|1999|pp=31-33}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Outline === In {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushi}}, {{jalink|Kaxiang|Q1373157}}3 years ({{jalink|850 years|Q30364}}) In addition to the article that "Orihatakami" is given the rank of Jigoinoge, {{jalink|Shinkai|Q11591025}}, {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} 3rd year ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) Junior Fifth Rank, {{jalink|Genkei|Q747451}}1st year ({{jalink|877|Q23905}}){{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.{{jalink|extension (era name)|Q1327274|extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927 years|Q30470}}) {{jali nk|Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{jalink|Chikuzen Province|Q1047664}}{{jalink|Munakata District|Q6935606}} has ``Orihata Shrine'' It is listed as {{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964}} along with Munakata Shrine 3 (currently Munakata Taisha) (Munakata County The only Shikinai shrines are Munakata Shrine and Orihata Shrine) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Among the place names that appear in "{{jalink|Wamyo Ruijusho|Q2702542|Wamyo Sho}}" from the middle of the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} According to one theory, this area is located in Kaifu-go, Munakata District. It is presumed that around this Heian period, Orihata Shrine became a shrine under the jurisdiction of Munakata Shrine.According to the Dajokan Code draft of {{jalink|Bun'ei|Q1010103}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1265|Q5516}}), at that time the {{jali nk|Chinkokuji|Q11651157}} {{jalink|Honjibutsu}} of ``Kanezaki Orihata Myojin'' was enshrined {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Also, in the {{jalink|Kishōmon|Q11636131}} of Bun'ei 9 ({{jalink|1272|Q5524}}), ``Orihata Daimyojin'' appears {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Established in {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}-{{jalink|1449|Q6446}}) In ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' the five gods of Munakata, plus Orihata Daimyojin and Kōgi Gongen, are collectively called the ``Munakata Five Shrines'' {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), {{j alink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} Ranked as {{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978 }}, in Meiji 15 ({{jalink|1882|Q7812}}), {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}, {{jalink|Showa|Q327 In 567}}3 ({{jalink|1928|Q19715}}), it was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978}}. In the 10th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1877|Q7773}}), Munakata Shrine (currently Munakata Taisha Shrine) reported it to the government as a separate shrine along with Matohara Shrine, Kodaiji Shrine, Miyajidake Shrine, and others. === God level === * July 29, 3rd year of {{jalink|Kasho|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|850 years|Q30364}}), Jugoinoge ("{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Emperor Annals|Q1052905}}") "Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals" July 29th Kasho 3rd year (850) article (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} January 27, 3rd year ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper junior fifth rank ("{{jalink|Japan Sandai Jiroki|Q1052981}}") "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated 27th New Year, Jogan 1st year (859) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * December 15th, {{jalink|Genkei|Q747451}} 1st year ({{jalink|877|Q23905}}), from Jugoinoge {{sub|Mama}} to Shogoikege (``Nihon Sandai Jirroku'') "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" December 15th, Genkei 1st year (877) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The name is written as "Orihatakami." === Shinto priest === The rituals at Orihata Shrine are said to have been carried out by {{jalink|Iki clan|Q11429628}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. According to legend, this Iki clan is said to be a descendant of the enshrined deity Maneko Iki, and when the Heike clan fell, they changed their surname from "Iki" to "Irie," but {{jalink|Bunkyu|Q7 It is said that it was returned to "Iki" in 30266}} ({{jalink|1863|Q7701}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. After that, the Iki family retired from the position of chief priest of Orihata Shrine in {{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}18 ({{jalink|1943|Q18623}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. === Precincts === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), shaden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Shrine building}}{{small|The main shrine is on the left, and the worship hall is on the right. }}]]The precincts are located halfway up Mt. Sayakata, at the tip of Cape Kaneno, which separates {{jalink|Genkai Sea|Q498047}} and {{jalink|Hibikinada|Q11288861}}. A temperate forest has formed within the grounds of Orihata Shrine and on Mt. Sayakata, including more than 10 giant trees of {{jalink|Inumaki|Q165063}}, {{jalink|Chii|Q1065400}}, {{jalink|Hamanabiwa|Q1258941} 4}}・{{jalink|Yabunikai|Q161335}}・{{jalink|Sangoju|Q12600081}}・{{jalink|Onore}}・{{jalink|Shirodamo|Q5792530}}・{{jalink|Tabnoki |Q5792073|Tab}}・{{jalink|Tobera|Q161375}}・{{jalink|Yatsude|Q203569}}・{{jalink|Tsubaki|Q160121}}・{{jalink|Yuzuriha|Q846968}}・{{ jalink|Yamamomo|Q311617}}・{{jalink|Sanekazura|Q1441971}}・{{jalink|Fouto Kazura|Q5924111}}・{{jalink|Sakaki|Q775470}}・{{jalink|Aoki (Plants) More than 20 species grow here, including |Q163114|Aoki}}, {{jalink|Masaki|Q161383}}, {{jalink|Kijoorchid|Q5367121|Kijoorchid}}, and {{jalink|Mukunoki|Q4779482}}. This natural forest has been designated as a natural monument of Fukuoka Prefecture as the ``Orihata Shrine Inumaki Natural Forest'' (Munahaku Electronic Museum). .According to ancient documents, the current main shrine was built in {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}8 ({{jalink|1695|Q7696}}), and the worship hall was built in Genroku 16 ({{jalink|1703|Q6822}}). Several other companies are also located within the precincts. Also within the precincts are Kutsuzuka, where the enshrined deity, Minister Takeuchi, buried the shoe left after ascending to heaven, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}, and a huge stone related to the legend of the sinking bell, which will be described later. File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-rin.jpg|Precinct forest (Fukuoka Prefecture designated natural monument) File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), Kutsu-zuka.jpg|Kutsuzuka File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-1.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-2.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Cultural assets === === Fukuoka Prefecture designated cultural property === * Natural monument ** Inumaki natural forest at Orihata Shrine - Designated on August 13, 1955Explanation board within the precincts. . === Tradition === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), kyoseki.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Giant stone of the legend of the sunken bell}}]] There is a legend that a foreign bell is sunk in the sea at Cape Kanenomisaki, where Orihata Shrine is located. The place name ``Cape Kaneno'' comes from this, and similar legends of sinking bells are known all over the country, but this area and Kanegasaki in {{jalink|Fukui Prefecture|Q133879}} are especially famous . However, what was thought to be a bell on the sea floor at Cape Kaneno was brought back to land in {{jalink|Taisho|Q329174}}8 ({{jalink|1919|Q2157}}), but what was pulled up at that time was not a bell but a megalith. This megalith is currently enshrined on the approach to Orihata Shrine. Additionally, the area between Cape Kaneno and {{jalink|Jijima|Q17992764}} on the opposite shore is a reef area, and it is known that it has been a key point and difficult point for maritime transportation since ancient times. "{{jalink|Manyoushu|Q834321}}" Manyoushu volume 7 number 1230 ([http://infux03.inf.edu.yamaguchi-u.ac.jp/~manyou/ver2_2/manyou_kekka2.php?kekka=07/1230 07/1230] reference). So,{{Cquote|Chihayaburu Even if I don't pass the golden cape, I won't forget Shiga's Emperor (Sumekami)|20px||"{{jalink|Man'yoshu|Q834321}}" Volume 7 No. 1230"New Edition of Japanese Classical Literature Complete Works 7 Man'yoshu (2)” Shogakukan, 2004 ({{jalink|Japan Knowledge|Q11309996}} edition), pp. 226-227. }} There is a song about praying to the god of the sea when passing Cape Kaneno (Kin no Misaki) {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Based on this song, there is a theory that Cape Kanenosaki was once inhabited by the Azum tribe, who worshiped the god Shiga. {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{jalink|Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258}}{{jalink|Munakata City|Q819603}}{{jalink|Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 '''Access''' * {{jalink|Nishitetsu Bus|Q11629843}}/Munakata City Community Bus get off at "Kyodomari" bus stop (short walk after getting off) - There are only a few buses. '''Surroundings''' * Kanezaki (Kamihachi) Shell Midden - A historic site designated by Munakata City. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Original text''' {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References/Sites === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board '''Literature''' * Published by Munakata Taisha ** {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[http://www.munakata-taisha.or.jp/infomation/singun01.pdf "Kamigun Munakata No. 1"]}} 2011, p. 8|ref={{Harvid|Kamigun Munakata No. 1|2011}}}}. ** {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[http://www.munakata-taisha.or.jp/infomation/singun04.pdf "Shingun Munakata No. 4"]}} 2013, pp. 6-7|ref={{Harvid|Shingun Munakata No. 4|2013}}}}. * Issued by local government ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=|title=Munakata City History General History Volume 2|publisher=Munakata City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Munakata City History General History Volume 2|1999}}}} * Encyclopedia ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Orihata Shrine|title={{jalink|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|Q4093011}} 40 Fukuoka Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765}}|isbn=978-4040014005|ref={{Harvid|Orihata Shrine (Kadokawa)|1988}}}}** {{Cite book|Japanese {{Wikicite|reference="Kanasaki/Kanezaki"|ref={{Harvid|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}}}. * Others ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Former {{jalink|Prefectural shrine}} |Founded = Emperor Richū year |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Alternative name = Orihata Shrine |Fudasho, etc. = Munakata Gosha|Regular Festival = {{jalink|April 16th|Q2519}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map+|Japan Fukuoka|width=200|float=center|caption=|places= {{Location map~|Japan Fukuoka|lat_deg=33|lat_min=49|lat_sec=52.37|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=51.21|position=bottom|label_size=85|label={{jalink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}}
(Hetsu Shrine)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=18}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukuoka|lat_deg=33|lat_min=53|lat_sec=16.05|lon_deg=130|lon_min=31|lon_sec=32.05|position=top|label_size=90|label='''Orihata Shrine'''|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}}}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}{{small|Mount Sayagata in the back. }}]] '''Orihata Shrine'' (Orihata Jinja) is located in {{jalink|Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258}}{{jalink|Munakata City|Q819603}}{{jalink|Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|Kanezaki}}. {{jalink|Shikinai Shrine}}, {{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964}}, and {{jalink|Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|old shrine rank}} is {{jalink|Prefectural shrine}}. Another name is "Orihataguu". Locals also call him ``Shikihan-sama''. === Enshrined God === The following seven deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Kamigun Munakata No. 1|2011|p=8}}. :* Minister Takeuchi (Takeuchi no Oomi) - {{jalink|Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668}} :* {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Three Gods|Q1114013|Sumiyoshi Okami}} (Sumiyoshi no Okami) :* Shiga Okami :* {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}} (Amaterasu no Okami) :* Munakata no Okami :* Kashii no Okami :* {{jalink|Hachimankami|Q261637|Hachiman Okami}} (Hachiman no Okami) :* {{jalink|Maneko Iki||Maneko Iki}}In "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}, which was established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), there is one enshrined deity. At the time of ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established during the {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} years ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}-{{jalink|1449|Q6446}}), it is written that Minister Takeuchi is the enshrined deity, and {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine )|2000}}, {{jalink|Early Modern Period|Q5308718}}'s ``{{jalink|Chikuzen Kunitsoku Fudoki|Q108571667}}'' states that the three deities enshrined here are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}. Currently, the eight deities listed above are enshrined, and the three main deities are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine History Book, Orihata Shrine|2025}}.During the {{jalink|Shinbutsu syncretization|Q2279088}} period, {{jalink|Honjibutsu}} was considered to be {{jalink|Nyoirin Kannon|Q11446712}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. {{jalink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}} Formerly {{jalink|Jinguji|Q1074971}} {{jalink|Chinkokuji|Q11651157}}, the original Buddhist statues of the five Munakata shrines (Fukuoka Prefecture-designated cultural properties) including Orihata Shrine have been preserved for generations. {{PDFlink|[http://city.munakata.lg.jp/w009/050/140/0010/130/13-14shinaisiteibunkazai-shuusei.pdf Cultural properties in the city]}} (Munakata City homepage). . === About the enshrined deity === The theory that the enshrined deity of Orihata Shrine is Takeuchi Sukune (Minister Takeuchi) dates back to ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established in the middle of the {{jalink|15th century|Q7018}}, and the same Engi records the founding tradition described below ({{jalink|#Sōken||Sōken-bushi}}). On the other hand, the precincts of Orihata Shrine are located at Cape Kaneno, a strategic point that juts out into the sea, and the entire area is also identified as ``Kaifu-go'' in ``{{jalink|Japanese name Ruijusho|Q2702542|Japanese namebook}}'', indicating that Kaifu existed there. Because of its location in the region, there is a theory that it was originally a sea god worshiped by the Kaijin tribe. As mentioned below ({{jalink|#tradition||tradition section}}), "{{jalink|Manyoshu|Q834321}}" When passing Cape Kaneno, there is a shrine called "Shiga no Kojin" ({{jalink|Shigaumi Shrine|Q11491171}}Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Aduji|Q3377806} }There is also a theory that the Azumi clan (Adu tribe), a member of the Kaijin tribe, resided in the area and worshiped the Shiga god, as there is a song that says they pray to the god Hosai.{{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}Also, "{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}" {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} era"Nihon Shoki" Article dated February 1, 1997 of Emperor Ojin, article of February 41, 41st year of Emperor Ojin. In , the Emperor ordered {{jalink|Achi Messenger|Q11657596}} and {{jalink|Tuka Messenger|Q24863564}} to go to Wu (Kangnan, China) and obtain sewing women, but when the Achi Messengers returned home, they were told that they had ``Chunkagata Okami'' (= There is a legend that he offered his older brother among the four female factory workers to the god Munakata Okami) at the request of the god Munakata Okami. , there is also a theory that connects this tradition with Orihata Shrine. Among these theories, it is pointed out that the weavers who weave clothes to offer to the gods may have been deified.{{Sfn|Munakata City History In General History Volume 2 | 1999 | pp=31-33}}, it is pointed out that Takeuchi Sukune may have been chosen as the enshrined deity as the common ancestor of the imported clans that were responsible for weaving {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods) | 2000}}.Of the eight deities enshrined, Maneko Iki is said to be the founder of the {{jalink|Iki clan|Q11429628}}, the former chief priest of Orihata Shrine {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. In addition to the existence of the Iki clan, the "Otenaga" that appears in the "Munakata Daibosatsu Engi" is {{jalink|Iki Island|Q494914}}'s Shikinai Shrine {{jalink|Amante Nagao Shrine|Q1 1442606}}・Since it is also associated with Amante Nagahime Shrine, there is a theory that suggests a relationship between the rituals at Orihata Shrine and Iki Island.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. In addition, there are different theories about the deity enshrined, such as the theory that it is Oomikutsu no Kami, and the theory that it is Kureori or Anaori.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}. === History === [[File:Kanezaki Port 01.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Distant view of Cape Kanezaki}}{{small|View from Kanezaki fishing port. Orihata Shrine is located halfway up Mt. Sayagata on the far left of the image. }}]] === Founding === Regarding its founding, according to "Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi" (established in the mid-15th century), during the {{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803}} {{jalink|Conquest of the Three Koreas|Q11249435}}, the Minister of Munakata ({{jal ink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}}) fought with two red and white flags woven by Minister Takeuchi ({{jalink|Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668}}) attached to a flagpole called ``Otenaga'', and in the end, they were used as ``Soku Mishima (= It was built on {{jalink|Okinoshima|Q7082047}}). At the time of Minister Takeuchi's funeral, the spirit was enshrined on the seashore to protect the sea route from invading pirates, and it was named ``Orihata (Orihata)'' because Minister Takeuchi wove the flag.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. The actual establishment is not known, but as mentioned above, there are theories that suggest a relationship with the Kaijin tribe, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Kadokawa)|1988}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}, "Nihon Shoki" and the legend of the seamstress of the Munakata deity.{{Sfn|Munakata City History General History Volume 2|1999|pp=31-33}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Outline === In {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushi}}, {{jalink|Kaxiang|Q1373157}}3 years ({{jalink|850 years|Q30364}}) In addition to the article that "Orihatakami" is given the rank of Jigoinoge, {{jalink|Shinkai|Q11591025}}, {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} 3rd year ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) Junior Fifth Rank, {{jalink|Genkei|Q747451}}1st year ({{jalink|877|Q23905}}){{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.{{jalink|extension (era name)|Q1327274|extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927 years|Q30470}}) {{jali nk|Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{jalink|Chikuzen Province|Q1047664}}{{jalink|Munakata District|Q6935606}} has ``Orihata Shrine'' It is listed as {{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964}} along with Munakata Shrine 3 (currently Munakata Taisha) (Munakata County The only Shikinai shrines are Munakata Shrine and Orihata Shrine) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Among the place names that appear in "{{jalink|Wamyo Ruijusho|Q2702542|Wamyo Sho}}" from the middle of the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} According to one theory, this area is located in Kaifu-go, Munakata District. It is presumed that around this Heian period, Orihata Shrine became a shrine under the jurisdiction of Munakata Shrine.According to the Dajokan Code draft of {{jalink|Bun'ei|Q1010103}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1265|Q5516}}), at that time the {{jali nk|Chinkokuji|Q11651157}} {{jalink|Honjibutsu}} of ``Kanezaki Orihata Myojin'' was enshrined {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Also, in the {{jalink|Kishōmon|Q11636131}} of Bun'ei 9 ({{jalink|1272|Q5524}}), ``Orihata Daimyojin'' appears {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Established in {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}-{{jalink|1449|Q6446}}) In ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' the five gods of Munakata, plus Orihata Daimyojin and Kōgi Gongen, are collectively called the ``Munakata Five Shrines'' {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), {{j alink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} Ranked as {{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978 }}, in Meiji 15 ({{jalink|1882|Q7812}}), {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}, {{jalink|Showa|Q327 In 567}}3 ({{jalink|1928|Q19715}}), it was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978}}. In the 10th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1877|Q7773}}), Munakata Shrine (currently Munakata Taisha Shrine) reported it to the government as a separate shrine along with Matohara Shrine, Kodaiji Shrine, Miyajidake Shrine, and others. === God level === * July 29, 3rd year of {{jalink|Kasho|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|850 years|Q30364}}), Jugoinoge ("{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Emperor Annals|Q1052905}}") "Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals" July 29th Kasho 3rd year (850) article (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} January 27, 3rd year ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper junior fifth rank ("{{jalink|Japan Sandai Jiroki|Q1052981}}") "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated 27th New Year, Jogan 1st year (859) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * December 15th, {{jalink|Genkei|Q747451}} 1st year ({{jalink|877|Q23905}}), from Jugoinoge {{sub|Mama}} to Shogoikege (``Nihon Sandai Jirroku'') "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" December 15th, Genkei 1st year (877) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The name is written as "Orihatakami." === Shinto priest === The rituals at Orihata Shrine are said to have been carried out by {{jalink|Iki clan|Q11429628}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. According to legend, this Iki clan is said to be a descendant of the enshrined deity Maneko Iki, and when the Heike clan fell, they changed their surname from "Iki" to "Irie," but {{jalink|Bunkyu|Q7 It is said that it was returned to "Iki" in 30266}} ({{jalink|1863|Q7701}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. After that, the Iki family retired from the position of chief priest of Orihata Shrine in {{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}18 ({{jalink|1943|Q18623}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. === Precincts === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), shaden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Shrine building}}{{small|The main shrine is on the left, and the worship hall is on the right. }}]]The precincts are located halfway up Mt. Sayakata, at the tip of Cape Kaneno, which separates {{jalink|Genkai Sea|Q498047}} and {{jalink|Hibikinada|Q11288861}}. A temperate forest has formed within the grounds of Orihata Shrine and on Mt. Sayakata, including more than 10 giant trees of {{jalink|Inumaki|Q165063}}, {{jalink|Chii|Q1065400}}, {{jalink|Hamanabiwa|Q1258941} 4}}・{{jalink|Yabunikai|Q161335}}・{{jalink|Sangoju|Q12600081}}・{{jalink|Onore}}・{{jalink|Shirodamo|Q5792530}}・{{jalink|Tabnoki |Q5792073|Tab}}・{{jalink|Tobera|Q161375}}・{{jalink|Yatsude|Q203569}}・{{jalink|Tsubaki|Q160121}}・{{jalink|Yuzuriha|Q846968}}・{{ jalink|Yamamomo|Q311617}}・{{jalink|Sanekazura|Q1441971}}・{{jalink|Fouto Kazura|Q5924111}}・{{jalink|Sakaki|Q775470}}・{{jalink|Aoki (Plants) More than 20 species grow here, including |Q163114|Aoki}}, {{jalink|Masaki|Q161383}}, {{jalink|Kijoorchid|Q5367121|Kijoorchid}}, and {{jalink|Mukunoki|Q4779482}}. This natural forest has been designated as a natural monument of Fukuoka Prefecture as the ``Orihata Shrine Inumaki Natural Forest'' (Munahaku Electronic Museum). .According to ancient documents, the current main shrine was built in {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}8 ({{jalink|1695|Q7696}}), and the worship hall was built in Genroku 16 ({{jalink|1703|Q6822}}). Several other companies are also located within the precincts. Also within the precincts are Kutsuzuka, where the enshrined deity, Minister Takeuchi, buried the shoe left after ascending to heaven, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}, and a huge stone related to the legend of the sinking bell, which will be described later. File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-rin.jpg|Precinct forest (Fukuoka Prefecture designated natural monument) File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), Kutsu-zuka.jpg|Kutsuzuka File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-1.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-2.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Cultural assets === === Fukuoka Prefecture designated cultural property === * Natural monument ** Inumaki natural forest at Orihata Shrine - Designated on August 13, 1955Explanation board within the precincts. . === Tradition === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), kyoseki.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Giant stone of the legend of the sunken bell}}]] There is a legend that a foreign bell is sunk in the sea at Cape Kanenomisaki, where Orihata Shrine is located. The place name ``Cape Kaneno'' comes from this, and similar legends of sinking bells are known all over the country, but this area and Kanegasaki in {{jalink|Fukui Prefecture|Q133879}} are especially famous . However, what was thought to be a bell on the sea floor at Cape Kaneno was brought back to land in {{jalink|Taisho|Q329174}}8 ({{jalink|1919|Q2157}}), but what was pulled up at that time was not a bell but a megalith. This megalith is currently enshrined on the approach to Orihata Shrine. Additionally, the area between Cape Kaneno and {{jalink|Jijima|Q17992764}} on the opposite shore is a reef area, and it is known that it has been a key point and difficult point for maritime transportation since ancient times. "{{jalink|Manyoushu|Q834321}}" Manyoushu volume 7 number 1230 ([http://infux03.inf.edu.yamaguchi-u.ac.jp/~manyou/ver2_2/manyou_kekka2.php?kekka=07/1230 07/1230] reference). So,{{Cquote|Chihayaburu Even if I don't pass the golden cape, I won't forget Shiga's Emperor (Sumekami)|20px||"{{jalink|Man'yoshu|Q834321}}" Volume 7 No. 1230"New Edition of Japanese Classical Literature Complete Works 7 Man'yoshu (2)” Shogakukan, 2004 ({{jalink|Japan Knowledge|Q11309996}} edition), pp. 226-227. }} There is a song about praying to the god of the sea when passing Cape Kaneno (Kin no Misaki) {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Based on this song, there is a theory that Cape Kanenosaki was once inhabited by the Azum tribe, who worshiped the god Shiga. {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{jalink|Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258}}{{jalink|Munakata City|Q819603}}{{jalink|Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 '''Access''' * {{jalink|Nishitetsu Bus|Q11629843}}/Munakata City Community Bus get off at "Kyodomari" bus stop (short walk after getting off) - There are only a few buses. '''Surroundings''' * Kanezaki (Kamihachi) Shell Midden - A historic site designated by Munakata City. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Original text''' {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References/Sites === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board '''Literature''' * Published by Munakata Taisha ** {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[http://www.munakata-taisha.or.jp/infomation/singun01.pdf "Kamigun Munakata No. 1"]}} 2011, p. 8|ref={{Harvid|Kamigun Munakata No. 1|2011}}}}. ** {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[http://www.munakata-taisha.or.jp/infomation/singun04.pdf "Shingun Munakata No. 4"]}} 2013, pp. 6-7|ref={{Harvid|Shingun Munakata No. 4|2013}}}}. * Issued by local government ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=|title=Munakata City History General History Volume 2|publisher=Munakata City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Munakata City History General History Volume 2|1999}}}} * Encyclopedia ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Orihata Shrine|title={{jalink|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|Q4093011}} 40 Fukuoka Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765}}|isbn=978-4040014005|ref={{Harvid|Orihata Shrine (Kadokawa)|1988}}}}** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=2004|chapter=|title={{jalink|Japanese historical place name system|Q17213509}} 41 Place names of Fukuoka prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490411|ref=}} *** {{Wikicite|reference="Orihata Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Kanasaki/Kanezaki"|ref={{Harvid|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}}}. * Others ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Orihata Shrine|title={{jalink|Prefecture Gosha Meiji Shrine Materials|Q11486103}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Materials|1912}}}} *** [{{NDLDC|1088313/574}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Part 2"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 574-575.** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Shikiuchisha Research Group|author1=Takashi Tanaka|authorlink1=Taku Tanaka|author2=Goro Mori|chapter=Orihata Shrine|year=1978|title=Shikiuchisha Research Report Volume 24|publisher={{jalink|Kogakukan University|Q1797584}}Publishing Department|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}}} ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Kenichi Tanikawa|editor-link=Kenichi Tanikawa|author=Masao Okuno|authorlink=Masao Okuno|year=2000|chapter=Orihata Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 1 Kyushu |publisher={{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}|isbn=4560025010|ref={{Harvid|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}}} '''Site''' * {{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/580301.html|author=|title=Orihata Shrine (Munakata District, Chikuzen Province)|work =|publisher=Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection”|date=|accessdate=2016-5-22|ref={{Harvid|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}}} === Related items === * {{jalink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}} === {{Expand Japanese|織幡神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Orihata Shrine | native_name = 織幡神社 | image = Orihata-jinja (Munakata), haiden.jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Orihata Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|33.88777|130.525564}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|de|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|es|Takenouchi no Sukune|fa|تاکنوشی نو سوکون|fr|Takenouchi no Sukune|ja|武内宿禰|ko|다케우치노 스쿠네|mni|ꯇꯀꯦꯅꯣꯎꯆꯤ ꯅꯣ ꯁꯨꯀꯨꯅꯦ|pt|Takenouchi no Sukune|zh|武內宿禰|WD=Q1073668}} }} {{nihongo|'''Orihata Shrine'''|織幡神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. haiden.jpg|270px]]
House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258}}{{jalink|Munakata City|Q819603}}{{jalink|Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|33|53|16.05|N|130|31|32.05|E|region:JP-39_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Orihata Shrine}} |Enshrined God = {{jalink|Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|Minister Takeuchi}}

(Hetsu Shrine)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=18}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukuoka|lat_deg=33|lat_min=53|lat_sec=16.05|lon_deg=130|lon_min=31|lon_sec=32.05|position=top|label_size=90|label='''Orihata Shrine'''|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}}}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}{{small|Mount Sayagata in the back. }}]] '''Orihata Shrine'' (Orihata Jinja) is located in {{jalink|Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258}}{{jalink|Munakata City|Q819603}}{{jalink|Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|Kanezaki}}. {{jalink|Shikinai Shrine}}, {{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964}}, and {{jalink|Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|old shrine rank}} is {{jalink|Prefectural shrine}}. Another name is "Orihataguu". Locals also call him ``Shikihan-sama''. === Enshrined God === The following seven deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Kamigun Munakata No. 1|2011|p=8}}. :* Minister Takeuchi (Takeuchi no Oomi) - {{jalink|Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668}} :* {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Three Gods|Q1114013|Sumiyoshi Okami}} (Sumiyoshi no Okami) :* Shiga Okami :* {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}} (Amaterasu no Okami) :* Munakata no Okami :* Kashii no Okami :* {{jalink|Hachimankami|Q261637|Hachiman Okami}} (Hachiman no Okami) :* {{jalink|Maneko Iki||Maneko Iki}}In "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}, which was established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), there is one enshrined deity. At the time of ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established during the {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} years ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}-{{jalink|1449|Q6446}}), it is written that Minister Takeuchi is the enshrined deity, and {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine )|2000}}, {{jalink|Early Modern Period|Q5308718}}'s ``{{jalink|Chikuzen Kunitsoku Fudoki|Q108571667}}'' states that the three deities enshrined here are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}. Currently, the eight deities listed above are enshrined, and the three main deities are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine History Book, Orihata Shrine|2025}}.During the {{jalink|Shinbutsu syncretization|Q2279088}} period, {{jalink|Honjibutsu}} was considered to be {{jalink|Nyoirin Kannon|Q11446712}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. {{jalink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}} Formerly {{jalink|Jinguji|Q1074971}} {{jalink|Chinkokuji|Q11651157}}, the original Buddhist statues of the five Munakata shrines (Fukuoka Prefecture-designated cultural properties) including Orihata Shrine have been preserved for generations. {{PDFlink|[http://city.munakata.lg.jp/w009/050/140/0010/130/13-14shinaisiteibunkazai-shuusei.pdf Cultural properties in the city]}} (Munakata City homepage). . === About the enshrined deity === The theory that the enshrined deity of Orihata Shrine is Takeuchi Sukune (Minister Takeuchi) dates back to ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established in the middle of the {{jalink|15th century|Q7018}}, and the same Engi records the founding tradition described below ({{jalink|#Sōken||Sōken-bushi}}). On the other hand, the precincts of Orihata Shrine are located at Cape Kaneno, a strategic point that juts out into the sea, and the entire area is also identified as ``Kaifu-go'' in ``{{jalink|Japanese name Ruijusho|Q2702542|Japanese namebook}}'', indicating that Kaifu existed there. Because of its location in the region, there is a theory that it was originally a sea god worshiped by the Kaijin tribe. As mentioned below ({{jalink|#tradition||tradition section}}), "{{jalink|Manyoshu|Q834321}}" When passing Cape Kaneno, there is a shrine called "Shiga no Kojin" ({{jalink|Shigaumi Shrine|Q11491171}}Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Aduji|Q3377806} }There is also a theory that the Azumi clan (Adu tribe), a member of the Kaijin tribe, resided in the area and worshiped the Shiga god, as there is a song that says they pray to the god Hosai.{{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}Also, "{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}" {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} era"Nihon Shoki" Article dated February 1, 1997 of Emperor Ojin, article of February 41, 41st year of Emperor Ojin. In , the Emperor ordered {{jalink|Achi Messenger|Q11657596}} and {{jalink|Tuka Messenger|Q24863564}} to go to Wu (Kangnan, China) and obtain sewing women, but when the Achi Messengers returned home, they were told that they had ``Chunkagata Okami'' (= There is a legend that he offered his older brother among the four female factory workers to the god Munakata Okami) at the request of the god Munakata Okami. , there is also a theory that connects this tradition with Orihata Shrine. Among these theories, it is pointed out that the weavers who weave clothes to offer to the gods may have been deified.{{Sfn|Munakata City History In General History Volume 2 | 1999 | pp=31-33}}, it is pointed out that Takeuchi Sukune may have been chosen as the enshrined deity as the common ancestor of the imported clans that were responsible for weaving {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods) | 2000}}.Of the eight deities enshrined, Maneko Iki is said to be the founder of the {{jalink|Iki clan|Q11429628}}, the former chief priest of Orihata Shrine {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. In addition to the existence of the Iki clan, the "Otenaga" that appears in the "Munakata Daibosatsu Engi" is {{jalink|Iki Island|Q494914}}'s Shikinai Shrine {{jalink|Amante Nagao Shrine|Q1 1442606}}・Since it is also associated with Amante Nagahime Shrine, there is a theory that suggests a relationship between the rituals at Orihata Shrine and Iki Island.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. In addition, there are different theories about the deity enshrined, such as the theory that it is Oomikutsu no Kami, and the theory that it is Kureori or Anaori.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}. === History === [[File:Kanezaki Port 01.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Distant view of Cape Kanezaki}}{{small|View from Kanezaki fishing port. Orihata Shrine is located halfway up Mt. Sayagata on the far left of the image. }}]] === Founding === Regarding its founding, according to "Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi" (established in the mid-15th century), during the {{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803}} {{jalink|Conquest of the Three Koreas|Q11249435}}, the Minister of Munakata ({{jal ink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}}) fought with two red and white flags woven by Minister Takeuchi ({{jalink|Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668}}) attached to a flagpole called ``Otenaga'', and in the end, they were used as ``Soku Mishima (= It was built on {{jalink|Okinoshima|Q7082047}}). At the time of Minister Takeuchi's funeral, the spirit was enshrined on the seashore to protect the sea route from invading pirates, and it was named ``Orihata (Orihata)'' because Minister Takeuchi wove the flag.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. The actual establishment is not known, but as mentioned above, there are theories that suggest a relationship with the Kaijin tribe, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Kadokawa)|1988}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}, "Nihon Shoki" and the legend of the seamstress of the Munakata deity.{{Sfn|Munakata City History General History Volume 2|1999|pp=31-33}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Outline === In {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushi}}, {{jalink|Kaxiang|Q1373157}}3 years ({{jalink|850 years|Q30364}}) In addition to the article that "Orihatakami" is given the rank of Jigoinoge, {{jalink|Shinkai|Q11591025}}, {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} 3rd year ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) Junior Fifth Rank, {{jalink|Genkei|Q747451}}1st year ({{jalink|877|Q23905}}){{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.{{jalink|extension (era name)|Q1327274|extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927 years|Q30470}}) {{jali nk|Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{jalink|Chikuzen Province|Q1047664}}{{jalink|Munakata District|Q6935606}} has ``Orihata Shrine'' It is listed as {{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964}} along with Munakata Shrine 3 (currently Munakata Taisha) (Munakata County The only Shikinai shrines are Munakata Shrine and Orihata Shrine) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Among the place names that appear in "{{jalink|Wamyo Ruijusho|Q2702542|Wamyo Sho}}" from the middle of the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} According to one theory, this area is located in Kaifu-go, Munakata District. It is presumed that around this Heian period, Orihata Shrine became a shrine under the jurisdiction of Munakata Shrine.According to the Dajokan Code draft of {{jalink|Bun'ei|Q1010103}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1265|Q5516}}), at that time the {{jali nk|Chinkokuji|Q11651157}} {{jalink|Honjibutsu}} of ``Kanezaki Orihata Myojin'' was enshrined {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Also, in the {{jalink|Kishōmon|Q11636131}} of Bun'ei 9 ({{jalink|1272|Q5524}}), ``Orihata Daimyojin'' appears {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Established in {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}-{{jalink|1449|Q6446}}) In ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' the five gods of Munakata, plus Orihata Daimyojin and Kōgi Gongen, are collectively called the ``Munakata Five Shrines'' {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), {{j alink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} Ranked as {{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978 }}, in Meiji 15 ({{jalink|1882|Q7812}}), {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}, {{jalink|Showa|Q327 In 567}}3 ({{jalink|1928|Q19715}}), it was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978}}. In the 10th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1877|Q7773}}), Munakata Shrine (currently Munakata Taisha Shrine) reported it to the government as a separate shrine along with Matohara Shrine, Kodaiji Shrine, Miyajidake Shrine, and others. === God level === * July 29, 3rd year of {{jalink|Kasho|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|850 years|Q30364}}), Jugoinoge ("{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Emperor Annals|Q1052905}}") "Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals" July 29th Kasho 3rd year (850) article (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} January 27, 3rd year ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper junior fifth rank ("{{jalink|Japan Sandai Jiroki|Q1052981}}") "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated 27th New Year, Jogan 1st year (859) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * December 15th, {{jalink|Genkei|Q747451}} 1st year ({{jalink|877|Q23905}}), from Jugoinoge {{sub|Mama}} to Shogoikege (``Nihon Sandai Jirroku'') "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" December 15th, Genkei 1st year (877) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The name is written as "Orihatakami." === Shinto priest === The rituals at Orihata Shrine are said to have been carried out by {{jalink|Iki clan|Q11429628}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. According to legend, this Iki clan is said to be a descendant of the enshrined deity Maneko Iki, and when the Heike clan fell, they changed their surname from "Iki" to "Irie," but {{jalink|Bunkyu|Q7 It is said that it was returned to "Iki" in 30266}} ({{jalink|1863|Q7701}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. After that, the Iki family retired from the position of chief priest of Orihata Shrine in {{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}18 ({{jalink|1943|Q18623}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. === Precincts === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), shaden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Shrine building}}{{small|The main shrine is on the left, and the worship hall is on the right. }}]]The precincts are located halfway up Mt. Sayakata, at the tip of Cape Kaneno, which separates {{jalink|Genkai Sea|Q498047}} and {{jalink|Hibikinada|Q11288861}}. A temperate forest has formed within the grounds of Orihata Shrine and on Mt. Sayakata, including more than 10 giant trees of {{jalink|Inumaki|Q165063}}, {{jalink|Chii|Q1065400}}, {{jalink|Hamanabiwa|Q1258941} 4}}・{{jalink|Yabunikai|Q161335}}・{{jalink|Sangoju|Q12600081}}・{{jalink|Onore}}・{{jalink|Shirodamo|Q5792530}}・{{jalink|Tabnoki |Q5792073|Tab}}・{{jalink|Tobera|Q161375}}・{{jalink|Yatsude|Q203569}}・{{jalink|Tsubaki|Q160121}}・{{jalink|Yuzuriha|Q846968}}・{{ jalink|Yamamomo|Q311617}}・{{jalink|Sanekazura|Q1441971}}・{{jalink|Fouto Kazura|Q5924111}}・{{jalink|Sakaki|Q775470}}・{{jalink|Aoki (Plants) More than 20 species grow here, including |Q163114|Aoki}}, {{jalink|Masaki|Q161383}}, {{jalink|Kijoorchid|Q5367121|Kijoorchid}}, and {{jalink|Mukunoki|Q4779482}}. This natural forest has been designated as a natural monument of Fukuoka Prefecture as the ``Orihata Shrine Inumaki Natural Forest'' (Munahaku Electronic Museum). .According to ancient documents, the current main shrine was built in {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}8 ({{jalink|1695|Q7696}}), and the worship hall was built in Genroku 16 ({{jalink|1703|Q6822}}). Several other companies are also located within the precincts. Also within the precincts are Kutsuzuka, where the enshrined deity, Minister Takeuchi, buried the shoe left after ascending to heaven, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}, and a huge stone related to the legend of the sinking bell, which will be described later. File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-rin.jpg|Precinct forest (Fukuoka Prefecture designated natural monument) File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), Kutsu-zuka.jpg|Kutsuzuka File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-1.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-2.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Cultural assets === === Fukuoka Prefecture designated cultural property === * Natural monument ** Inumaki natural forest at Orihata Shrine - Designated on August 13, 1955Explanation board within the precincts. . === Tradition === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), kyoseki.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Giant stone of the legend of the sunken bell}}]] There is a legend that a foreign bell is sunk in the sea at Cape Kanenomisaki, where Orihata Shrine is located. The place name ``Cape Kaneno'' comes from this, and similar legends of sinking bells are known all over the country, but this area and Kanegasaki in {{jalink|Fukui Prefecture|Q133879}} are especially famous . However, what was thought to be a bell on the sea floor at Cape Kaneno was brought back to land in {{jalink|Taisho|Q329174}}8 ({{jalink|1919|Q2157}}), but what was pulled up at that time was not a bell but a megalith. This megalith is currently enshrined on the approach to Orihata Shrine. Additionally, the area between Cape Kaneno and {{jalink|Jijima|Q17992764}} on the opposite shore is a reef area, and it is known that it has been a key point and difficult point for maritime transportation since ancient times. "{{jalink|Manyoushu|Q834321}}" Manyoushu volume 7 number 1230 ([http://infux03.inf.edu.yamaguchi-u.ac.jp/~manyou/ver2_2/manyou_kekka2.php?kekka=07/1230 07/1230] reference). So,{{Cquote|Chihayaburu Even if I don't pass the golden cape, I won't forget Shiga's Emperor (Sumekami)|20px||"{{jalink|Man'yoshu|Q834321}}" Volume 7 No. 1230"New Edition of Japanese Classical Literature Complete Works 7 Man'yoshu (2)” Shogakukan, 2004 ({{jalink|Japan Knowledge|Q11309996}} edition), pp. 226-227. }} There is a song about praying to the god of the sea when passing Cape Kaneno (Kin no Misaki) {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Based on this song, there is a theory that Cape Kanenosaki was once inhabited by the Azum tribe, who worshiped the god Shiga. {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{jalink|Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258}}{{jalink|Munakata City|Q819603}}{{jalink|Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 '''Access''' * {{jalink|Nishitetsu Bus|Q11629843}}/Munakata City Community Bus get off at "Kyodomari" bus stop (short walk after getting off) - There are only a few buses. '''Surroundings''' * Kanezaki (Kamihachi) Shell Midden - A historic site designated by Munakata City. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Original text''' {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References/Sites === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board '''Literature''' * Published by Munakata Taisha ** {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[http://www.munakata-taisha.or.jp/infomation/singun04.pdf "Shingun Munakata No. 4"]}} 2013, pp. 6-7|ref={{Harvid|Shingun Munakata No. 4|2013}}}}. * Issued by local government ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=|title=Munakata City History General History Volume 2|publisher=Munakata City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Munakata City History General History Volume 2|1999}}}} * Encyclopedia ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Orihata Shrine|title={{jalink|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|Q4093011}} 40 Fukuoka {{Wikicite|reference="Kanasaki/Kanezaki"|ref={{Harvid|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}}}. * Others ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial
