haiden.jpg|270px]]
House of worship |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258}}{{ill|lt=Munakata City|Q819603|WD=Q819603}}{{ill|lt=Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|WD=Q11651333|lt=Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|33|53|16.05|N|130|31|32.05|E|region:JP-39_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Orihata Shrine}} |Enshrined God = {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|lt=Minister Takeuchi}}
{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Three Gods|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi Okami}}
Shiga Okami
{{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}}
Munakata Okami
{{ill|lt=Kashiikami||WD=|lt=Kashii Okami}}
{{ill|lt=Hachimankami|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman Okami}}
{{ill|lt=Maneko Iki||WD=|lt=Maneko Iki}} |Divinity = |Company status = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Meishin University}})
Former {{ill||lt=Prefectural shrine|UNKNOWN}} |Founded = Emperor Richū year |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternative name = Orihata Shrine |Fudasho, etc. = Munakata Gosha|Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=April 16th|Q2519|WD=Q2519}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map+|Japan Fukuoka|width=200|float=center|caption=|places= {{Location map~|Japan Fukuoka|lat_deg=33|lat_min=49|lat_sec=52.37|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=51.21|position=bottom|label_size=85|label={{ill|lt=Munakata Taisha|Q704962|WD=Q704962}}
(Hetsu Shrine)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=18}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukuoka|lat_deg=33|lat_min=53|lat_sec=16.05|lon_deg=130|lon_min=31|lon_sec=32.05|position=top|label_size=90|label='''Orihata Shrine'''|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}}}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}{{small|Mount Sayagata in the back. }}]] '''Orihata Shrine'' (Orihata Jinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258}}{{ill|lt=Munakata City|Q819603|WD=Q819603}}{{ill|lt=Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|WD=Q11651333|lt=Kanezaki}}. {{ill||lt=Shikinai Shrine|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}, and {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old shrine rank}} is {{ill||lt=Prefectural shrine|UNKNOWN}}. Another name is "Orihataguu". Locals also call him ``Shikihan-sama''. === Enshrined God === The following seven deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Kamigun Munakata No. 1|2011|p=8}}. :* Minister Takeuchi (Takeuchi no Oomi) - {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}} :* {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Three Gods|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi Okami}} (Sumiyoshi no Okami) :* Shiga Okami :* {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}} (Amaterasu no Okami) :* Munakata no Okami :* Kashii no Okami :* {{ill|lt=Hachimankami|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman Okami}} (Hachiman no Okami) :* {{ill|lt=Maneko Iki||WD=|lt=Maneko Iki}}In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}}, which was established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), there is one enshrined deity. At the time of ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established during the {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} years ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}-{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}), it is written that Minister Takeuchi is the enshrined deity, and {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine )|2000}}, {{ill|lt=Early Modern Period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}'s ``{{ill|lt=Chikuzen Kunitsoku Fudoki|Q108571667|WD=Q108571667}}'' states that the three deities enshrined here are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}. Currently, the eight deities listed above are enshrined, and the three main deities are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine History Book, Orihata Shrine|2025}}.During the {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu syncretization|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088}} period, {{ill||lt=Honjibutsu|UNKNOWN}} was considered to be {{ill|lt=Nyoirin Kannon|Q11446712|WD=Q11446712}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. {{ill|lt=Munakata Taisha|Q704962|WD=Q704962}} Formerly {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}} {{ill|lt=Chinkokuji|Q11651157|WD=Q11651157}}, the original Buddhist statues of the five Munakata shrines (Fukuoka Prefecture-designated cultural properties) including Orihata Shrine have been preserved for generations. {{PDFlink|[http://city.munakata.lg.jp/w009/050/140/0010/130/13-14shinaisiteibunkazai-shuusei.pdf Cultural properties in the city]}} (Munakata City homepage). . === About the enshrined deity === The theory that the enshrined deity of Orihata Shrine is Takeuchi Sukune (Minister Takeuchi) dates back to ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established in the middle of the {{ill|lt=15th century|Q7018|WD=Q7018}}, and the same Engi records the founding tradition described below ({{ill|lt=#Sōken||WD=|lt=Sōken-bushi}}). On the other hand, the precincts of Orihata Shrine are located at Cape Kaneno, a strategic point that juts out into the sea, and the entire area is also identified as ``Kaifu-go'' in ``{{ill|lt=Japanese name Ruijusho|Q2702542|WD=Q2702542|lt=Japanese namebook}}'', indicating that Kaifu existed there. Because of its location in the region, there is a theory that it was originally a sea god worshiped by the Kaijin tribe. As mentioned below ({{ill|lt=#tradition||WD=|lt=tradition section}}), "{{ill|lt=Manyoshu|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}" When passing Cape Kaneno, there is a shrine called "Shiga no Kojin" ({{ill|lt=Shigaumi Shrine|Q11491171|WD=Q11491171}}Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Aduji|Q3377806} }There is also a theory that the Azumi clan (Adu tribe), a member of the Kaijin tribe, resided in the area and worshiped the Shiga god, as there is a song that says they pray to the god Hosai.{{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}Also, "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}" {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} era"Nihon Shoki" Article dated February 1, 1997 of Emperor Ojin, article of February 41, 41st year of Emperor Ojin. In , the Emperor ordered {{ill|lt=Achi Messenger|Q11657596|WD=Q11657596}} and {{ill|lt=Tuka Messenger|Q24863564|WD=Q24863564}} to go to Wu (Kangnan, China) and obtain sewing women, but when the Achi Messengers returned home, they were told that they had ``Chunkagata Okami'' (= There is a legend that he offered his older brother among the four female factory workers to the god Munakata Okami) at the request of the god Munakata Okami. , there is also a theory that connects this tradition with Orihata Shrine. Among these theories, it is pointed out that the weavers who weave clothes to offer to the gods may have been deified.{{Sfn|Munakata City History In General History Volume 2 | 1999 | pp=31-33}}, it is pointed out that Takeuchi Sukune may have been chosen as the enshrined deity as the common ancestor of the imported clans that were responsible for weaving {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods) | 2000}}.Of the eight deities enshrined, Maneko Iki is said to be the founder of the {{ill|lt=Iki clan|Q11429628|WD=Q11429628}}, the former chief priest of Orihata Shrine {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. In addition to the existence of the Iki clan, the "Otenaga" that appears in the "Munakata Daibosatsu Engi" is {{ill|lt=Iki Island|Q494914|WD=Q494914}}'s Shikinai Shrine {{ill||lt=Amante Nagao Shrine|UNKNOWN}}・Since it is also associated with Amante Nagahime Shrine, there is a theory that suggests a relationship between the rituals at Orihata Shrine and Iki Island.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. In addition, there are different theories about the deity enshrined, such as the theory that it is Oomikutsu no Kami, and the theory that it is Kureori or Anaori.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}. === History === [[File:Kanezaki Port 01.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Distant view of Cape Kanezaki}}{{small|View from Kanezaki fishing port. Orihata Shrine is located halfway up Mt. Sayagata on the far left of the image. }}]] === Founding === Regarding its founding, according to "Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi" (established in the mid-15th century), during the {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} {{ill|lt=Conquest of the Three Koreas|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}}, the Minister of Munakata ({{jal ink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}}) fought with two red and white flags woven by Minister Takeuchi ({{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}}) attached to a flagpole called ``Otenaga'', and in the end, they were used as ``Soku Mishima (= It was built on {{ill|lt=Okinoshima|Q7082047|WD=Q7082047}}). At the time of Minister Takeuchi's funeral, the spirit was enshrined on the seashore to protect the sea route from invading pirates, and it was named ``Orihata (Orihata)'' because Minister Takeuchi wove the flag.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. The actual establishment is not known, but as mentioned above, there are theories that suggest a relationship with the Kaijin tribe, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Kadokawa)|1988}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}, "Nihon Shoki" and the legend of the seamstress of the Munakata deity.{{Sfn|Munakata City History General History Volume 2|1999|pp=31-33}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Outline === In {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokushi}}, {{ill|lt=Kaxiang|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}}3 years ({{ill|lt=850 years|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}) In addition to the article that "Orihatakami" is given the rank of Jigoinoge, {{ill|lt=Shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}, {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}) Junior Fifth Rank, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451}}1st year ({{ill|lt=877|Q23905|WD=Q23905}}){{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.{{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=extended}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) {{jali nk|Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{ill|lt=Chikuzen Province|Q1047664|WD=Q1047664}}{{ill|lt=Munakata District|Q6935606|WD=Q6935606}} has ``Orihata Shrine'' It is listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}} along with Munakata Shrine 3 (currently Munakata Taisha) (Munakata County The only Shikinai shrines are Munakata Shrine and Orihata Shrine) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Among the place names that appear in "{{ill|lt=Wamyo Ruijusho|Q2702542|WD=Q2702542|lt=Wamyo Sho}}" from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} According to one theory, this area is located in Kaifu-go, Munakata District. It is presumed that around this Heian period, Orihata Shrine became a shrine under the jurisdiction of Munakata Shrine.According to the Dajokan Code draft of {{ill|lt=Bun'ei|Q1010103|WD=Q1010103}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1265|Q5516|WD=Q5516}}), at that time the {{jali nk|Chinkokuji|Q11651157}} {{ill||lt=Honjibutsu|UNKNOWN}} of ``Kanezaki Orihata Myojin'' was enshrined {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Also, in the {{ill|lt=Kishōmon|Q11636131|WD=Q11636131}} of Bun'ei 9 ({{ill|lt=1272|Q5524|WD=Q5524}}), ``Orihata Daimyojin'' appears {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Established in {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}-{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}) In ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' the five gods of Munakata, plus Orihata Daimyojin and Kōgi Gongen, are collectively called the ``Munakata Five Shrines'' {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), {{j alink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} Ranked as {{ill|lt=Murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978 }}, in Meiji 15 ({{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812}}), {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}, {{ill||lt=Showa|UNKNOWN}}3 ({{ill|lt=1928|Q19715|WD=Q19715}}), it was promoted to {{ill||lt=prefectural shrine|UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978}}. In the 10th year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1877|Q7773|WD=Q7773}}), Munakata Shrine (currently Munakata Taisha Shrine) reported it to the government as a separate shrine along with Matohara Shrine, Kodaiji Shrine, Miyajidake Shrine, and others. === God level === * July 29, 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Kasho|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} ({{ill|lt=850 years|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}), Jugoinoge ("{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Emperor Annals|Q1052905|WD=Q1052905}}") "Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals" July 29th Kasho 3rd year (850) article (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} January 27, 3rd year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper junior fifth rank ("{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jiroki|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}") "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated 27th New Year, Jogan 1st year (859) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * December 15th, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=877|Q23905|WD=Q23905}}), from Jugoinoge {{sub|Mama}} to Shogoikege (``Nihon Sandai Jirroku'') "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" December 15th, Genkei 1st year (877) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The name is written as "Orihatakami." === Shinto priest === The rituals at Orihata Shrine are said to have been carried out by {{ill|lt=Iki clan|Q11429628|WD=Q11429628}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. According to legend, this Iki clan is said to be a descendant of the enshrined deity Maneko Iki, and when the Heike clan fell, they changed their surname from "Iki" to "Irie," but {{ill||lt=Bunkyu|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=1863|Q7701|WD=Q7701}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. After that, the Iki family retired from the position of chief priest of Orihata Shrine in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}}18 ({{ill|lt=1943|Q18623|WD=Q18623}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. === Precincts === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), shaden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Shrine building}}{{small|The main shrine is on the left, and the worship hall is on the right. }}]]The precincts are located halfway up Mt. Sayakata, at the tip of Cape Kaneno, which separates {{ill|lt=Genkai Sea|Q498047|WD=Q498047}} and {{ill|lt=Hibikinada|Q11288861|WD=Q11288861}}. A temperate forest has formed within the grounds of Orihata Shrine and on Mt. Sayakata, including more than 10 giant trees of {{ill|lt=Inumaki|Q165063|WD=Q165063}}, {{ill|lt=Chii|Q1065400|WD=Q1065400}}, {{jalink|Hamanabiwa|Q1258941} 4}}・{{ill|lt=Yabunikai|Q161335|WD=Q161335}}・{{ill|lt=Sangoju|Q12600081|WD=Q12600081}}・{{ill||lt=Onore|UNKNOWN}}・{{ill|lt=Shirodamo|Q5792530|WD=Q5792530}}・{{ill|lt=Tabnoki|Q5792073|WD=Q5792073|lt=Tab}}・{{ill|lt=Tobera|Q161375|WD=Q161375}}・{{ill|lt=Yatsude|Q203569|WD=Q203569}}・{{ill|lt=Tsubaki|Q160121|WD=Q160121}}・{{ill|lt=Yuzuriha|Q846968|WD=Q846968}}・{{ jalink|Yamamomo|Q311617}}・{{ill|lt=Sanekazura|Q1441971|WD=Q1441971}}・{{ill|lt=Fouto Kazura|Q5924111|WD=Q5924111}}・{{ill|lt=Sakaki|Q775470|WD=Q775470}}・{{ill|lt=Aoki (Plants) More than 20 species grow here, including|Q163114|WD=Q163114|lt=Aoki}}, {{ill|lt=Masaki|Q161383|WD=Q161383}}, {{ill|lt=Kijoorchid|Q5367121|WD=Q5367121|lt=Kijoorchid}}, and {{ill|lt=Mukunoki|Q4779482|WD=Q4779482}}. This natural forest has been designated as a natural monument of Fukuoka Prefecture as the ``Orihata Shrine Inumaki Natural Forest'' (Munahaku Electronic Museum). .According to ancient documents, the current main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}8 ({{ill|lt=1695|Q7696|WD=Q7696}}), and the worship hall was built in Genroku 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822}}). Several other companies are also located within the precincts. Also within the precincts are Kutsuzuka, where the enshrined deity, Minister Takeuchi, buried the shoe left after ascending to heaven, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}, and a huge stone related to the legend of the sinking bell, which will be described later. File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-rin.jpg|Precinct forest (Fukuoka Prefecture designated natural monument) File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), Kutsu-zuka.jpg|Kutsuzuka File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-1.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-2.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Cultural assets === === Fukuoka Prefecture designated cultural property === * Natural monument ** Inumaki natural forest at Orihata Shrine - Designated on August 13, 1955Explanation board within the precincts. . === Tradition === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), kyoseki.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Giant stone of the legend of the sunken bell}}]] There is a legend that a foreign bell is sunk in the sea at Cape Kanenomisaki, where Orihata Shrine is located. The place name ``Cape Kaneno'' comes from this, and similar legends of sinking bells are known all over the country, but this area and Kanegasaki in {{ill|lt=Fukui Prefecture|Q133879|WD=Q133879}} are especially famous . However, what was thought to be a bell on the sea floor at Cape Kaneno was brought back to land in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174}}8 ({{ill|lt=1919|Q2157|WD=Q2157}}), but what was pulled up at that time was not a bell but a megalith. This megalith is currently enshrined on the approach to Orihata Shrine. Additionally, the area between Cape Kaneno and {{ill|lt=Jijima|Q17992764|WD=Q17992764}} on the opposite shore is a reef area, and it is known that it has been a key point and difficult point for maritime transportation since ancient times. "{{ill|lt=Manyoushu|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}" Manyoushu volume 7 number 1230 ([http://infux03.inf.edu.yamaguchi-u.ac.jp/~manyou/ver2_2/manyou_kekka2.php?kekka=07/1230 07/1230] reference). So,{{Cquote|Chihayaburu Even if I don't pass the golden cape, I won't forget Shiga's Emperor (Sumekami)|20px||"{{ill|lt=Man'yoshu|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}" Volume 7 No. 1230"New Edition of Japanese Classical Literature Complete Works 7 Man'yoshu (2)” Shogakukan, 2004 ({{ill|lt=Japan Knowledge|Q11309996|WD=Q11309996}} edition), pp. 226-227. }} There is a song about praying to the god of the sea when passing Cape Kaneno (Kin no Misaki) {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Based on this song, there is a theory that Cape Kanenosaki was once inhabited by the Azum tribe, who worshiped the god Shiga. {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258}}{{ill|lt=Munakata City|Q819603|WD=Q819603}}{{ill|lt=Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|WD=Q11651333|lt=Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 '''Access''' * {{ill|lt=Nishitetsu Bus|Q11629843|WD=Q11629843}}/Munakata City Community Bus get off at "Kyodomari" bus stop (short walk after getting off) - There are only a few buses. '''Surroundings''' * Kanezaki (Kamihachi) Shell Midden - A historic site designated by Munakata City. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Original text''' {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' (Hetsu Shrine)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=18}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukuoka|lat_deg=33|lat_min=53|lat_sec=16.05|lon_deg=130|lon_min=31|lon_sec=32.05|position=top|label_size=90|label='''Orihata Shrine'''|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}}}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}{{small|Mount Sayagata in the back. }}]] '''Orihata Shrine'' (Orihata Jinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258}}{{ill|lt=Munakata City|Q819603|WD=Q819603}}{{ill|lt=Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|WD=Q11651333|lt=Kanezaki}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai Shrine|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}, and {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old shrine rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|UNKNOWN}}. Another name is "Orihataguu". Locals also call him ``Shikihan-sama''. === Enshrined God === The following seven deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Kamigun Munakata No. 1|2011|p=8}}. :* Minister Takeuchi (Takeuchi no Oomi) - {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}} :* {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Three Gods|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi Okami}} (Sumiyoshi no Okami) :* Shiga Okami :* {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}} (Amaterasu no Okami) :* Munakata no Okami :* Kashii no Okami :* {{ill|lt=Hachimankami|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman Okami}} (Hachiman no Okami) :* {{ill|lt=Maneko Iki||WD=|lt=Maneko Iki}}In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}}, which was established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), there is one enshrined deity. At the time of ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established during the {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} years ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}-{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}), it is written that Minister Takeuchi is the enshrined deity, and {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine )|2000}}, {{ill|lt=Early Modern Period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}'s ``{{ill|lt=Chikuzen Kunitsoku Fudoki|Q108571667|WD=Q108571667}}'' states that the three deities enshrined here are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}. Currently, the eight deities listed above are enshrined, and the three main deities are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine History Book, Orihata Shrine|2025}}.During the {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu syncretization|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088}} period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Honjibutsu|UNKNOWN}} was considered to be {{ill|lt=Nyoirin Kannon|Q11446712|WD=Q11446712}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. {{ill|lt=Munakata Taisha|Q704962|WD=Q704962}} Formerly {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}} {{ill|lt=Chinkokuji|Q11651157|WD=Q11651157}}, the original Buddhist statues of the five Munakata shrines (Fukuoka Prefecture-designated cultural properties) including Orihata Shrine have been preserved for generations. {{PDFlink|[http://city.munakata.lg.jp/w009/050/140/0010/130/13-14shinaisiteibunkazai-shuusei.pdf Cultural properties in the city]}} (Munakata City homepage). . === About the enshrined deity === The theory that the enshrined deity of Orihata Shrine is Takeuchi Sukune (Minister Takeuchi) dates back to ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established in the middle of the {{ill|lt=15th century|Q7018|WD=Q7018}}, and the same Engi records the founding tradition described below ({{ill|lt=#Sōken||WD=|lt=Sōken-bushi}}). On the other hand, the precincts of Orihata Shrine are located at Cape Kaneno, a strategic point that juts out into the sea, and the entire area is also identified as ``Kaifu-go'' in ``{{ill|lt=Japanese name Ruijusho|Q2702542|WD=Q2702542|lt=Japanese namebook}}'', indicating that Kaifu existed there. Because of its location in the region, there is a theory that it was originally a sea god worshiped by the Kaijin tribe. As mentioned below ({{ill|lt=#tradition||WD=|lt=tradition section}}), "{{ill|lt=Manyoshu|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}" When passing Cape Kaneno, there is a shrine called "Shiga no Kojin" ({{ill|lt=Shigaumi Shrine|Q11491171|WD=Q11491171}}Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Aduji|Q3377806} }There is also a theory that the Azumi clan (Adu tribe), a member of the Kaijin tribe, resided in the area and worshiped the Shiga god, as there is a song that says they pray to the god Hosai.{{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}Also, "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}" {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} era"Nihon Shoki" Article dated February 1, 1997 of Emperor Ojin, article of February 41, 41st year of Emperor Ojin. In , the Emperor ordered {{ill|lt=Achi Messenger|Q11657596|WD=Q11657596}} and {{ill|lt=Tuka Messenger|Q24863564|WD=Q24863564}} to go to Wu (Kangnan, China) and obtain sewing women, but when the Achi Messengers returned home, they were told that they had ``Chunkagata Okami'' (= There is a legend that he offered his older brother among the four female factory workers to the god Munakata Okami) at the request of the god Munakata Okami. , there is also a theory that connects this tradition with Orihata Shrine. Among these theories, it is pointed out that the weavers who weave clothes to offer to the gods may have been deified.{{Sfn|Munakata City History In General History Volume 2 | 1999 | pp=31-33}}, it is pointed out that Takeuchi Sukune may have been chosen as the enshrined deity as the common ancestor of the imported clans that were responsible for weaving {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods) | 2000}}.Of the eight deities enshrined, Maneko Iki is said to be the founder of the {{ill|lt=Iki clan|Q11429628|WD=Q11429628}}, the former chief priest of Orihata Shrine {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. In addition to the existence of the Iki clan, the "Otenaga" that appears in the "Munakata Daibosatsu Engi" is {{ill|lt=Iki Island|Q494914|WD=Q494914}}'s Shikinai Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amante Nagao Shrine|UNKNOWN}}・Since it is also associated with Amante Nagahime Shrine, there is a theory that suggests a relationship between the rituals at Orihata Shrine and Iki Island.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. In addition, there are different theories about the deity enshrined, such as the theory that it is Oomikutsu no Kami, and the theory that it is Kureori or Anaori.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}. === History === [[File:Kanezaki Port 01.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Distant view of Cape Kanezaki}}{{small|View from Kanezaki fishing port. Orihata Shrine is located halfway up Mt. Sayagata on the far left of the image. }}]] === Founding === Regarding its founding, according to "Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi" (established in the mid-15th century), during the {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} {{ill|lt=Conquest of the Three Koreas|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}}, the Minister of Munakata ({{jal ink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}}) fought with two red and white flags woven by Minister Takeuchi ({{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}}) attached to a flagpole called ``Otenaga'', and in the end, they were used as ``Soku Mishima (= It was built on {{ill|lt=Okinoshima|Q7082047|WD=Q7082047}}). At the time of Minister Takeuchi's funeral, the spirit was enshrined on the seashore to protect the sea route from invading pirates, and it was named ``Orihata (Orihata)'' because Minister Takeuchi wove the flag.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. The actual establishment is not known, but as mentioned above, there are theories that suggest a relationship with the Kaijin tribe, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Kadokawa)|1988}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}, "Nihon Shoki" and the legend of the seamstress of the Munakata deity.{{Sfn|Munakata City History General History Volume 2|1999|pp=31-33}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Outline === In {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokushi}}, {{ill|lt=Kaxiang|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}}3 years ({{ill|lt=850 years|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}) In addition to the article that "Orihatakami" is given the rank of Jigoinoge, {{ill|lt=Shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}, {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}) Junior Fifth Rank, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451}}1st year ({{ill|lt=877|Q23905|WD=Q23905}}){{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.{{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=extended}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) {{jali nk|Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{ill|lt=Chikuzen Province|Q1047664|WD=Q1047664}}{{ill|lt=Munakata District|Q6935606|WD=Q6935606}} has ``Orihata Shrine'' It is listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}} along with Munakata Shrine 3 (currently Munakata Taisha) (Munakata County The only Shikinai shrines are Munakata Shrine and Orihata Shrine) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Among the place names that appear in "{{ill|lt=Wamyo Ruijusho|Q2702542|WD=Q2702542|lt=Wamyo Sho}}" from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} According to one theory, this area is located in Kaifu-go, Munakata District. It is presumed that around this Heian period, Orihata Shrine became a shrine under the jurisdiction of Munakata Shrine.According to the Dajokan Code draft of {{ill|lt=Bun'ei|Q1010103|WD=Q1010103}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1265|Q5516|WD=Q5516}}), at that time the {{jali nk|Chinkokuji|Q11651157}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Honjibutsu|UNKNOWN}} of ``Kanezaki Orihata Myojin'' was enshrined {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Also, in the {{ill|lt=Kishōmon|Q11636131|WD=Q11636131}} of Bun'ei 9 ({{ill|lt=1272|Q5524|WD=Q5524}}), ``Orihata Daimyojin'' appears {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Established in {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}-{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}) In ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' the five gods of Munakata, plus Orihata Daimyojin and Kōgi Gongen, are collectively called the ``Munakata Five Shrines'' {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), {{j alink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} Ranked as {{ill|lt=Murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978 }}, in Meiji 15 ({{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812}}), {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Showa|UNKNOWN}}3 ({{ill|lt=1928|Q19715|WD=Q19715}}), it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978}}. In the 10th year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1877|Q7773|WD=Q7773}}), Munakata Shrine (currently Munakata Taisha Shrine) reported it to the government as a separate shrine along with Matohara Shrine, Kodaiji Shrine, Miyajidake Shrine, and others. === God level === * July 29, 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Kasho|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} ({{ill|lt=850 years|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}), Jugoinoge ("{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Emperor Annals|Q1052905|WD=Q1052905}}") "Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals" July 29th Kasho 3rd year (850) article (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} January 27, 3rd year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper junior fifth rank ("{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jiroki|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}") "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated 27th New Year, Jogan 1st year (859) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * December 15th, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=877|Q23905|WD=Q23905}}), from Jugoinoge {{sub|Mama}} to Shogoikege (``Nihon Sandai Jirroku'') "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" December 15th, Genkei 1st year (877) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The name is written as "Orihatakami." === Shinto priest === The rituals at Orihata Shrine are said to have been carried out by {{ill|lt=Iki clan|Q11429628|WD=Q11429628}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. According to legend, this Iki clan is said to be a descendant of the enshrined deity Maneko Iki, and when the Heike clan fell, they changed their surname from "Iki" to "Irie," but {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Bunkyu|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=1863|Q7701|WD=Q7701}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. After that, the Iki family retired from the position of chief priest of Orihata Shrine in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}}18 ({{ill|lt=1943|Q18623|WD=Q18623}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. === Precincts === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), shaden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Shrine building}}{{small|The main shrine is on the left, and the worship hall is on the right. }}]]The precincts are located halfway up Mt. Sayakata, at the tip of Cape Kaneno, which separates {{ill|lt=Genkai Sea|Q498047|WD=Q498047}} and {{ill|lt=Hibikinada|Q11288861|WD=Q11288861}}. A temperate forest has formed within the grounds of Orihata Shrine and on Mt. Sayakata, including more than 10 giant trees of {{ill|lt=Inumaki|Q165063|WD=Q165063}}, {{ill|lt=Chii|Q1065400|WD=Q1065400}}, {{jalink|Hamanabiwa|Q1258941} 4}}・{{ill|lt=Yabunikai|Q161335|WD=Q161335}}・{{ill|lt=Sangoju|Q12600081|WD=Q12600081}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onore|UNKNOWN}}・{{ill|lt=Shirodamo|Q5792530|WD=Q5792530}}・{{ill|lt=Tabnoki|Q5792073|WD=Q5792073|lt=Tab}}・{{ill|lt=Tobera|Q161375|WD=Q161375}}・{{ill|lt=Yatsude|Q203569|WD=Q203569}}・{{ill|lt=Tsubaki|Q160121|WD=Q160121}}・{{ill|lt=Yuzuriha|Q846968|WD=Q846968}}・{{ jalink|Yamamomo|Q311617}}・{{ill|lt=Sanekazura|Q1441971|WD=Q1441971}}・{{ill|lt=Fouto Kazura|Q5924111|WD=Q5924111}}・{{ill|lt=Sakaki|Q775470|WD=Q775470}}・{{ill|lt=Aoki (Plants) More than 20 species grow here, including|Q163114|WD=Q163114|lt=Aoki}}, {{ill|lt=Masaki|Q161383|WD=Q161383}}, {{ill|lt=Kijoorchid|Q5367121|WD=Q5367121|lt=Kijoorchid}}, and {{ill|lt=Mukunoki|Q4779482|WD=Q4779482}}. This natural forest has been designated as a natural monument of Fukuoka Prefecture as the ``Orihata Shrine Inumaki Natural Forest'' (Munahaku Electronic Museum). .According to ancient documents, the current main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}8 ({{ill|lt=1695|Q7696|WD=Q7696}}), and the worship hall was built in Genroku 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822}}). Several other companies are also located within the precincts. Also within the precincts are Kutsuzuka, where the enshrined deity, Minister Takeuchi, buried the shoe left after ascending to heaven, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}, and a huge stone related to the legend of the sinking bell, which will be described later. File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-rin.jpg|Precinct forest (Fukuoka Prefecture designated natural monument) File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), Kutsu-zuka.jpg|Kutsuzuka File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-1.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-2.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Cultural assets === === Fukuoka Prefecture designated cultural property === * Natural monument ** Inumaki natural forest at Orihata Shrine - Designated on August 13, 1955Explanation board within the precincts. . === Tradition === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), kyoseki.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Giant stone of the legend of the sunken bell}}]] There is a legend that a foreign bell is sunk in the sea at Cape Kanenomisaki, where Orihata Shrine is located. The place name ``Cape Kaneno'' comes from this, and similar legends of sinking bells are known all over the country, but this area and Kanegasaki in {{ill|lt=Fukui Prefecture|Q133879|WD=Q133879}} are especially famous . However, what was thought to be a bell on the sea floor at Cape Kaneno was brought back to land in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174}}8 ({{ill|lt=1919|Q2157|WD=Q2157}}), but what was pulled up at that time was not a bell but a megalith. This megalith is currently enshrined on the approach to Orihata Shrine. Additionally, the area between Cape Kaneno and {{ill|lt=Jijima|Q17992764|WD=Q17992764}} on the opposite shore is a reef area, and it is known that it has been a key point and difficult point for maritime transportation since ancient times. "{{ill|lt=Manyoushu|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}" Manyoushu volume 7 number 1230 ([http://infux03.inf.edu.yamaguchi-u.ac.jp/~manyou/ver2_2/manyou_kekka2.php?kekka=07/1230 07/1230] reference). So,{{Cquote|Chihayaburu Even if I don't pass the golden cape, I won't forget Shiga's Emperor (Sumekami)|20px||"{{ill|lt=Man'yoshu|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}" Volume 7 No. 1230"New Edition of Japanese Classical Literature Complete Works 7 Man'yoshu (2)” Shogakukan, 2004 ({{ill|lt=Japan Knowledge|Q11309996|WD=Q11309996}} edition), pp. 226-227. }} There is a song about praying to the god of the sea when passing Cape Kaneno (Kin no Misaki) {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Based on this song, there is a theory that Cape Kanenosaki was once inhabited by the Azum tribe, who worshiped the god Shiga. {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258}}{{ill|lt=Munakata City|Q819603|WD=Q819603}}{{ill|lt=Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|WD=Q11651333|lt=Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 '''Access''' * {{ill|lt=Nishitetsu Bus|Q11629843|WD=Q11629843}}/Munakata City Community Bus get off at "Kyodomari" bus stop (short walk after getting off) - There are only a few buses. '''Surroundings''' * Kanezaki (Kamihachi) Shell Midden - A historic site designated by Munakata City. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Original text''' {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' haiden.jpg|270px]]
House of worship |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258}}{{ill|lt=Munakata City|Q819603|WD=Q819603}}{{ill|lt=Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|WD=Q11651333|lt=Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|33|53|16.05|N|130|31|32.05|E|region:JP-39_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Orihata Shrine}} |Enshrined God = {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|lt=Minister Takeuchi}}
{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Three Gods|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi Okami}}
Shiga Okami
{{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}}
Munakata Okami
{{ill|lt=Kashiikami||WD=|lt=Kashii Okami}}
{{ill|lt=Hachimankami|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman Okami}}
{{ill|lt=Maneko Iki||WD=|lt=Maneko Iki}} |Divinity = |Company status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Meishin University}})
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded = Emperor Richū year |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternative name = Orihata Shrine |Fudasho, etc. = Munakata Gosha|Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=April 16th|Q2519|WD=Q2519}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map+|Japan Fukuoka|width=200|float=center|caption=|places= {{Location map~|Japan Fukuoka|lat_deg=33|lat_min=49|lat_sec=52.37|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=51.21|position=bottom|label_size=85|label={{ill|lt=Munakata Taisha|Q704962|WD=Q704962}}
(Hetsu Shrine)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=18}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukuoka|lat_deg=33|lat_min=53|lat_sec=16.05|lon_deg=130|lon_min=31|lon_sec=32.05|position=top|label_size=90|label='''Orihata Shrine'''|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}}}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}{{small|Mount Sayagata in the back. }}]] '''Orihata Shrine'' (Orihata Jinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258}}{{ill|lt=Munakata City|Q819603|WD=Q819603}}{{ill|lt=Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|WD=Q11651333|lt=Kanezaki}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}, and {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old shrine rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Another name is "Orihataguu". Locals also call him ``Shikihan-sama''. === Enshrined God === The following seven deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Kamigun Munakata No. 1|2011|p=8}}. :* Minister Takeuchi (Takeuchi no Oomi) - {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}} :* {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Three Gods|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi Okami}} (Sumiyoshi no Okami) :* Shiga Okami :* {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}} (Amaterasu no Okami) :* Munakata no Okami :* Kashii no Okami :* {{ill|lt=Hachimankami|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman Okami}} (Hachiman no Okami) :* {{ill|lt=Maneko Iki||WD=|lt=Maneko Iki}}In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}}, which was established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), there is one enshrined deity. At the time of ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established during the {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} years ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}-{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}), it is written that Minister Takeuchi is the enshrined deity, and {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine )|2000}}, {{ill|lt=Early Modern Period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}'s ``{{ill|lt=Chikuzen Kunitsoku Fudoki|Q108571667|WD=Q108571667}}'' states that the three deities enshrined here are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}. Currently, the eight deities listed above are enshrined, and the three main deities are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine History Book, Orihata Shrine|2025}}.During the {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu syncretization|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088}} period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Honjibutsu|1=UNKNOWN}} was considered to be {{ill|lt=Nyoirin Kannon|Q11446712|WD=Q11446712}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. {{ill|lt=Munakata Taisha|Q704962|WD=Q704962}} Formerly {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}} {{ill|lt=Chinkokuji|Q11651157|WD=Q11651157}}, the original Buddhist statues of the five Munakata shrines (Fukuoka Prefecture-designated cultural properties) including Orihata Shrine have been preserved for generations. {{PDFlink|[http://city.munakata.lg.jp/w009/050/140/0010/130/13-14shinaisiteibunkazai-shuusei.pdf Cultural properties in the city]}} (Munakata City homepage). . === About the enshrined deity === The theory that the enshrined deity of Orihata Shrine is Takeuchi Sukune (Minister Takeuchi) dates back to ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established in the middle of the {{ill|lt=15th century|Q7018|WD=Q7018}}, and the same Engi records the founding tradition described below ({{ill|lt=#Sōken||WD=|lt=Sōken-bushi}}). On the other hand, the precincts of Orihata Shrine are located at Cape Kaneno, a strategic point that juts out into the sea, and the entire area is also identified as ``Kaifu-go'' in ``{{ill|lt=Japanese name Ruijusho|Q2702542|WD=Q2702542|lt=Japanese namebook}}'', indicating that Kaifu existed there. Because of its location in the region, there is a theory that it was originally a sea god worshiped by the Kaijin tribe. As mentioned below ({{ill|lt=#tradition||WD=|lt=tradition section}}), "{{ill|lt=Manyoshu|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}" When passing Cape Kaneno, there is a shrine called "Shiga no Kojin" ({{ill|lt=Shigaumi Shrine|Q11491171|WD=Q11491171}}Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Aduji|Q3377806} }There is also a theory that the Azumi clan (Adu tribe), a member of the Kaijin tribe, resided in the area and worshiped the Shiga god, as there is a song that says they pray to the god Hosai.{{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}Also, "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}" {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} era"Nihon Shoki" Article dated February 1, 1997 of Emperor Ojin, article of February 41, 41st year of Emperor Ojin. In , the Emperor ordered {{ill|lt=Achi Messenger|Q11657596|WD=Q11657596}} and {{ill|lt=Tuka Messenger|Q24863564|WD=Q24863564}} to go to Wu (Kangnan, China) and obtain sewing women, but when the Achi Messengers returned home, they were told that they had ``Chunkagata Okami'' (= There is a legend that he offered his older brother among the four female factory workers to the god Munakata Okami) at the request of the god Munakata Okami. , there is also a theory that connects this tradition with Orihata Shrine. Among these theories, it is pointed out that the weavers who weave clothes to offer to the gods may have been deified.{{Sfn|Munakata City History In General History Volume 2 | 1999 | pp=31-33}}, it is pointed out that Takeuchi Sukune may have been chosen as the enshrined deity as the common ancestor of the imported clans that were responsible for weaving {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods) | 2000}}.Of the eight deities enshrined, Maneko Iki is said to be the founder of the {{ill|lt=Iki clan|Q11429628|WD=Q11429628}}, the former chief priest of Orihata Shrine {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. In addition to the existence of the Iki clan, the "Otenaga" that appears in the "Munakata Daibosatsu Engi" is {{ill|lt=Iki Island|Q494914|WD=Q494914}}'s Shikinai Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amante Nagao Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}・Since it is also associated with Amante Nagahime Shrine, there is a theory that suggests a relationship between the rituals at Orihata Shrine and Iki Island.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. In addition, there are different theories about the deity enshrined, such as the theory that it is Oomikutsu no Kami, and the theory that it is Kureori or Anaori.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}. === History === [[File:Kanezaki Port 01.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Distant view of Cape Kanezaki}}{{small|View from Kanezaki fishing port. Orihata Shrine is located halfway up Mt. Sayagata on the far left of the image. }}]] === Founding === Regarding its founding, according to "Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi" (established in the mid-15th century), during the {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} {{ill|lt=Conquest of the Three Koreas|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}}, the Minister of Munakata ({{jal ink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}}) fought with two red and white flags woven by Minister Takeuchi ({{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}}) attached to a flagpole called ``Otenaga'', and in the end, they were used as ``Soku Mishima (= It was built on {{ill|lt=Okinoshima|Q7082047|WD=Q7082047}}). At the time of Minister Takeuchi's funeral, the spirit was enshrined on the seashore to protect the sea route from invading pirates, and it was named ``Orihata (Orihata)'' because Minister Takeuchi wove the flag.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. The actual establishment is not known, but as mentioned above, there are theories that suggest a relationship with the Kaijin tribe, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Kadokawa)|1988}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}, "Nihon Shoki" and the legend of the seamstress of the Munakata deity.{{Sfn|Munakata City History General History Volume 2|1999|pp=31-33}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Outline === In {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokushi}}, {{ill|lt=Kaxiang|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}}3 years ({{ill|lt=850 years|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}) In addition to the article that "Orihatakami" is given the rank of Jigoinoge, {{ill|lt=Shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}, {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}) Junior Fifth Rank, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451}}1st year ({{ill|lt=877|Q23905|WD=Q23905}}){{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.{{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=extended}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) {{jali nk|Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{ill|lt=Chikuzen Province|Q1047664|WD=Q1047664}}{{ill|lt=Munakata District|Q6935606|WD=Q6935606}} has ``Orihata Shrine'' It is listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}} along with Munakata Shrine 3 (currently Munakata Taisha) (Munakata County The only Shikinai shrines are Munakata Shrine and Orihata Shrine) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Among the place names that appear in "{{ill|lt=Wamyo Ruijusho|Q2702542|WD=Q2702542|lt=Wamyo Sho}}" from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} According to one theory, this area is located in Kaifu-go, Munakata District. It is presumed that around this Heian period, Orihata Shrine became a shrine under the jurisdiction of Munakata Shrine.According to the Dajokan Code draft of {{ill|lt=Bun'ei|Q1010103|WD=Q1010103}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1265|Q5516|WD=Q5516}}), at that time the {{jali nk|Chinkokuji|Q11651157}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Honjibutsu|1=UNKNOWN}} of ``Kanezaki Orihata Myojin'' was enshrined {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Also, in the {{ill|lt=Kishōmon|Q11636131|WD=Q11636131}} of Bun'ei 9 ({{ill|lt=1272|Q5524|WD=Q5524}}), ``Orihata Daimyojin'' appears {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Established in {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}-{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}) In ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' the five gods of Munakata, plus Orihata Daimyojin and Kōgi Gongen, are collectively called the ``Munakata Five Shrines'' {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), {{j alink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} Ranked as {{ill|lt=Murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978 }}, in Meiji 15 ({{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812}}), {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Showa|1=UNKNOWN}}3 ({{ill|lt=1928|Q19715|WD=Q19715}}), it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978}}. In the 10th year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1877|Q7773|WD=Q7773}}), Munakata Shrine (currently Munakata Taisha Shrine) reported it to the government as a separate shrine along with Matohara Shrine, Kodaiji Shrine, Miyajidake Shrine, and others. === God level === * July 29, 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Kasho|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} ({{ill|lt=850 years|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}), Jugoinoge ("{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Emperor Annals|Q1052905|WD=Q1052905}}") "Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals" July 29th Kasho 3rd year (850) article (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} January 27, 3rd year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper junior fifth rank ("{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jiroki|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}") "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated 27th New Year, Jogan 1st year (859) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * December 15th, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=877|Q23905|WD=Q23905}}), from Jugoinoge {{sub|Mama}} to Shogoikege (``Nihon Sandai Jirroku'') "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" December 15th, Genkei 1st year (877) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The name is written as "Orihatakami." === Shinto priest === The rituals at Orihata Shrine are said to have been carried out by {{ill|lt=Iki clan|Q11429628|WD=Q11429628}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. According to legend, this Iki clan is said to be a descendant of the enshrined deity Maneko Iki, and when the Heike clan fell, they changed their surname from "Iki" to "Irie," but {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Bunkyu|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=1863|Q7701|WD=Q7701}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. After that, the Iki family retired from the position of chief priest of Orihata Shrine in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}}18 ({{ill|lt=1943|Q18623|WD=Q18623}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. === Precincts === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), shaden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Shrine building}}{{small|The main shrine is on the left, and the worship hall is on the right. }}]]The precincts are located halfway up Mt. Sayakata, at the tip of Cape Kaneno, which separates {{ill|lt=Genkai Sea|Q498047|WD=Q498047}} and {{ill|lt=Hibikinada|Q11288861|WD=Q11288861}}. A temperate forest has formed within the grounds of Orihata Shrine and on Mt. Sayakata, including more than 10 giant trees of {{ill|lt=Inumaki|Q165063|WD=Q165063}}, {{ill|lt=Chii|Q1065400|WD=Q1065400}}, {{jalink|Hamanabiwa|Q1258941} 4}}・{{ill|lt=Yabunikai|Q161335|WD=Q161335}}・{{ill|lt=Sangoju|Q12600081|WD=Q12600081}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onore|1=UNKNOWN}}・{{ill|lt=Shirodamo|Q5792530|WD=Q5792530}}・{{ill|lt=Tabnoki|Q5792073|WD=Q5792073|lt=Tab}}・{{ill|lt=Tobera|Q161375|WD=Q161375}}・{{ill|lt=Yatsude|Q203569|WD=Q203569}}・{{ill|lt=Tsubaki|Q160121|WD=Q160121}}・{{ill|lt=Yuzuriha|Q846968|WD=Q846968}}・{{ jalink|Yamamomo|Q311617}}・{{ill|lt=Sanekazura|Q1441971|WD=Q1441971}}・{{ill|lt=Fouto Kazura|Q5924111|WD=Q5924111}}・{{ill|lt=Sakaki|Q775470|WD=Q775470}}・{{ill|lt=Aoki (Plants) More than 20 species grow here, including|Q163114|WD=Q163114|lt=Aoki}}, {{ill|lt=Masaki|Q161383|WD=Q161383}}, {{ill|lt=Kijoorchid|Q5367121|WD=Q5367121|lt=Kijoorchid}}, and {{ill|lt=Mukunoki|Q4779482|WD=Q4779482}}. This natural forest has been designated as a natural monument of Fukuoka Prefecture as the ``Orihata Shrine Inumaki Natural Forest'' (Munahaku Electronic Museum). .According to ancient documents, the current main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}8 ({{ill|lt=1695|Q7696|WD=Q7696}}), and the worship hall was built in Genroku 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822}}). Several other companies are also located within the precincts. Also within the precincts are Kutsuzuka, where the enshrined deity, Minister Takeuchi, buried the shoe left after ascending to heaven, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}, and a huge stone related to the legend of the sinking bell, which will be described later. File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-rin.jpg|Precinct forest (Fukuoka Prefecture designated natural monument) File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), Kutsu-zuka.jpg|Kutsuzuka File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-1.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-2.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Cultural assets === === Fukuoka Prefecture designated cultural property === * Natural monument ** Inumaki natural forest at Orihata Shrine - Designated on August 13, 1955Explanation board within the precincts. . === Tradition === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), kyoseki.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Giant stone of the legend of the sunken bell}}]] There is a legend that a foreign bell is sunk in the sea at Cape Kanenomisaki, where Orihata Shrine is located. The place name ``Cape Kaneno'' comes from this, and similar legends of sinking bells are known all over the country, but this area and Kanegasaki in {{ill|lt=Fukui Prefecture|Q133879|WD=Q133879}} are especially famous . However, what was thought to be a bell on the sea floor at Cape Kaneno was brought back to land in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174}}8 ({{ill|lt=1919|Q2157|WD=Q2157}}), but what was pulled up at that time was not a bell but a megalith. This megalith is currently enshrined on the approach to Orihata Shrine. Additionally, the area between Cape Kaneno and {{ill|lt=Jijima|Q17992764|WD=Q17992764}} on the opposite shore is a reef area, and it is known that it has been a key point and difficult point for maritime transportation since ancient times. "{{ill|lt=Manyoushu|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}" Manyoushu volume 7 number 1230 ([http://infux03.inf.edu.yamaguchi-u.ac.jp/~manyou/ver2_2/manyou_kekka2.php?kekka=07/1230 07/1230] reference). So,{{Cquote|Chihayaburu Even if I don't pass the golden cape, I won't forget Shiga's Emperor (Sumekami)|20px||"{{ill|lt=Man'yoshu|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}" Volume 7 No. 1230"New Edition of Japanese Classical Literature Complete Works 7 Man'yoshu (2)” Shogakukan, 2004 ({{ill|lt=Japan Knowledge|Q11309996|WD=Q11309996}} edition), pp. 226-227. }} There is a song about praying to the god of the sea when passing Cape Kaneno (Kin no Misaki) {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Based on this song, there is a theory that Cape Kanenosaki was once inhabited by the Azum tribe, who worshiped the god Shiga. {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258}}{{ill|lt=Munakata City|Q819603|WD=Q819603}}{{ill|lt=Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|WD=Q11651333|lt=Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 '''Access''' * {{ill|lt=Nishitetsu Bus|Q11629843|WD=Q11629843}}/Munakata City Community Bus get off at "Kyodomari" bus stop (short walk after getting off) - There are only a few buses. '''Surroundings''' * Kanezaki (Kamihachi) Shell Midden - A historic site designated by Munakata City. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Original text''' {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' (Hetsu Shrine)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=18}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukuoka|lat_deg=33|lat_min=53|lat_sec=16.05|lon_deg=130|lon_min=31|lon_sec=32.05|position=top|label_size=90|label='''Orihata Shrine'''|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}}}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}{{small|Mount Sayagata in the back. }}]] '''Orihata Shrine'' (Orihata Jinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258}}{{ill|lt=Munakata City|Q819603|WD=Q819603}}{{ill|lt=Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|WD=Q11651333|lt=Kanezaki}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}, and {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old shrine rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Another name is "Orihataguu". Locals also call him ``Shikihan-sama''. === Enshrined God === The following seven deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Kamigun Munakata No. 1|2011|p=8}}. :* Minister Takeuchi (Takeuchi no Oomi) - {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}} :* {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Three Gods|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi Okami}} (Sumiyoshi no Okami) :* Shiga Okami :* {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}} (Amaterasu no Okami) :* Munakata no Okami :* Kashii no Okami :* {{ill|lt=Hachimankami|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman Okami}} (Hachiman no Okami) :* {{ill|lt=Maneko Iki|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Maneko Iki}}In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}}, which was established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), there is one enshrined deity. At the time of ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established during the {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} years ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}-{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}), it is written that Minister Takeuchi is the enshrined deity, and {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine )|2000}}, {{ill|lt=Early Modern Period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}'s ``{{ill|lt=Chikuzen Kunitsoku Fudoki|Q108571667|WD=Q108571667}}'' states that the three deities enshrined here are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}. Currently, the eight deities listed above are enshrined, and the three main deities are Takeuchi Minister, Sumiyoshi Okami, and Shiga Okami {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine History Book, Orihata Shrine|2025}}.During the {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu syncretization|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088}} period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Honjibutsu|1=UNKNOWN}} was considered to be {{ill|lt=Nyoirin Kannon|Q11446712|WD=Q11446712}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. {{ill|lt=Munakata Taisha|Q704962|WD=Q704962}} Formerly {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}} {{ill|lt=Chinkokuji|Q11651157|WD=Q11651157}}, the original Buddhist statues of the five Munakata shrines (Fukuoka Prefecture-designated cultural properties) including Orihata Shrine have been preserved for generations. {{PDFlink|[http://city.munakata.lg.jp/w009/050/140/0010/130/13-14shinaisiteibunkazai-shuusei.pdf Cultural properties in the city]}} (Munakata City homepage). . === About the enshrined deity === The theory that the enshrined deity of Orihata Shrine is Takeuchi Sukune (Minister Takeuchi) dates back to ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' which was established in the middle of the {{ill|lt=15th century|Q7018|WD=Q7018}}, and the same Engi records the founding tradition described below ({{ill|lt=#Sōken|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Sōken-bushi}}). On the other hand, the precincts of Orihata Shrine are located at Cape Kaneno, a strategic point that juts out into the sea, and the entire area is also identified as ``Kaifu-go'' in ``{{ill|lt=Japanese name Ruijusho|Q2702542|WD=Q2702542|lt=Japanese namebook}}'', indicating that Kaifu existed there. Because of its location in the region, there is a theory that it was originally a sea god worshiped by the Kaijin tribe. As mentioned below ({{ill|lt=#tradition|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=tradition section}}), "{{ill|lt=Manyoshu|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}" When passing Cape Kaneno, there is a shrine called "Shiga no Kojin" ({{ill|lt=Shigaumi Shrine|Q11491171|WD=Q11491171}}Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Aduji|Q3377806} }There is also a theory that the Azumi clan (Adu tribe), a member of the Kaijin tribe, resided in the area and worshiped the Shiga god, as there is a song that says they pray to the god Hosai.{{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}Also, "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}" {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} era"Nihon Shoki" Article dated February 1, 1997 of Emperor Ojin, article of February 41, 41st year of Emperor Ojin. In , the Emperor ordered {{ill|lt=Achi Messenger|Q11657596|WD=Q11657596}} and {{ill|lt=Tuka Messenger|Q24863564|WD=Q24863564}} to go to Wu (Kangnan, China) and obtain sewing women, but when the Achi Messengers returned home, they were told that they had ``Chunkagata Okami'' (= There is a legend that he offered his older brother among the four female factory workers to the god Munakata Okami) at the request of the god Munakata Okami. , there is also a theory that connects this tradition with Orihata Shrine. Among these theories, it is pointed out that the weavers who weave clothes to offer to the gods may have been deified.{{Sfn|Munakata City History In General History Volume 2 | 1999 | pp=31-33}}, it is pointed out that Takeuchi Sukune may have been chosen as the enshrined deity as the common ancestor of the imported clans that were responsible for weaving {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods) | 2000}}.Of the eight deities enshrined, Maneko Iki is said to be the founder of the {{ill|lt=Iki clan|Q11429628|WD=Q11429628}}, the former chief priest of Orihata Shrine {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. In addition to the existence of the Iki clan, the "Otenaga" that appears in the "Munakata Daibosatsu Engi" is {{ill|lt=Iki Island|Q494914|WD=Q494914}}'s Shikinai Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amante Nagao Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}・Since it is also associated with Amante Nagahime Shrine, there is a theory that suggests a relationship between the rituals at Orihata Shrine and Iki Island.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. In addition, there are different theories about the deity enshrined, such as the theory that it is Oomikutsu no Kami, and the theory that it is Kureori or Anaori.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}. === History === [[File:Kanezaki Port 01.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Distant view of Cape Kanezaki}}{{small|View from Kanezaki fishing port. Orihata Shrine is located halfway up Mt. Sayagata on the far left of the image. }}]] === Founding === Regarding its founding, according to "Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi" (established in the mid-15th century), during the {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} {{ill|lt=Conquest of the Three Koreas|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}}, the Minister of Munakata ({{jal ink|Munakata Taisha|Q704962}}) fought with two red and white flags woven by Minister Takeuchi ({{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}}) attached to a flagpole called ``Otenaga'', and in the end, they were used as ``Soku Mishima (= It was built on {{ill|lt=Okinoshima|Q7082047|WD=Q7082047}}). At the time of Minister Takeuchi's funeral, the spirit was enshrined on the seashore to protect the sea route from invading pirates, and it was named ``Orihata (Orihata)'' because Minister Takeuchi wove the flag.{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. The actual establishment is not known, but as mentioned above, there are theories that suggest a relationship with the Kaijin tribe, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Kadokawa)|1988}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}, "Nihon Shoki" and the legend of the seamstress of the Munakata deity.{{Sfn|Munakata City History General History Volume 2|1999|pp=31-33}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Gods)|2000}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Outline === In {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokushi}}, {{ill|lt=Kaxiang|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}}3 years ({{ill|lt=850 years|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}) In addition to the article that "Orihatakami" is given the rank of Jigoinoge, {{ill|lt=Shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}, {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}) Junior Fifth Rank, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451}}1st year ({{ill|lt=877|Q23905|WD=Q23905}}){{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.{{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=extended}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) {{jali nk|Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{ill|lt=Chikuzen Province|Q1047664|WD=Q1047664}}{{ill|lt=Munakata District|Q6935606|WD=Q6935606}} has ``Orihata Shrine'' It is listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}} along with Munakata Shrine 3 (currently Munakata Taisha) (Munakata County The only Shikinai shrines are Munakata Shrine and Orihata Shrine) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Among the place names that appear in "{{ill|lt=Wamyo Ruijusho|Q2702542|WD=Q2702542|lt=Wamyo Sho}}" from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} According to one theory, this area is located in Kaifu-go, Munakata District. It is presumed that around this Heian period, Orihata Shrine became a shrine under the jurisdiction of Munakata Shrine.According to the Dajokan Code draft of {{ill|lt=Bun'ei|Q1010103|WD=Q1010103}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1265|Q5516|WD=Q5516}}), at that time the {{jali nk|Chinkokuji|Q11651157}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Honjibutsu|1=UNKNOWN}} of ``Kanezaki Orihata Myojin'' was enshrined {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Also, in the {{ill|lt=Kishōmon|Q11636131|WD=Q11636131}} of Bun'ei 9 ({{ill|lt=1272|Q5524|WD=Q5524}}), ``Orihata Daimyojin'' appears {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Established in {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}-{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}) In ``Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi,'' the five gods of Munakata, plus Orihata Daimyojin and Kōgi Gongen, are collectively called the ``Munakata Five Shrines'' {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), {{j alink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} Ranked as {{ill|lt=Murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978 }}, in Meiji 15 ({{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812}}), {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Showa|1=UNKNOWN}}3 ({{ill|lt=1928|Q19715|WD=Q19715}}), it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1978}}. In the 10th year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1877|Q7773|WD=Q7773}}), Munakata Shrine (currently Munakata Taisha Shrine) reported it to the government as a separate shrine along with Matohara Shrine, Kodaiji Shrine, Miyajidake Shrine, and others. === God level === * July 29, 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Kasho|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} ({{ill|lt=850 years|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}), Jugoinoge ("{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Emperor Annals|Q1052905|WD=Q1052905}}") "Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals" July 29th Kasho 3rd year (850) article (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} January 27, 3rd year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper junior fifth rank ("{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jiroki|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}") "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated 27th New Year, Jogan 1st year (859) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The spelling is "Orihatakami". * December 15th, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=877|Q23905|WD=Q23905}}), from Jugoinoge {{sub|Mama}} to Shogoikege (``Nihon Sandai Jirroku'') "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" December 15th, Genkei 1st year (877) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The name is written as "Orihatakami." === Shinto priest === The rituals at Orihata Shrine are said to have been carried out by {{ill|lt=Iki clan|Q11429628|WD=Q11429628}} {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1978}}. According to legend, this Iki clan is said to be a descendant of the enshrined deity Maneko Iki, and when the Heike clan fell, they changed their surname from "Iki" to "Irie," but {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Bunkyu|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=1863|Q7701|WD=Q7701}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. After that, the Iki family retired from the position of chief priest of Orihata Shrine in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}}18 ({{ill|lt=1943|Q18623|WD=Q18623}}) {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}. === Precincts === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), shaden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Shrine building}}{{small|The main shrine is on the left, and the worship hall is on the right. }}]]The precincts are located halfway up Mt. Sayakata, at the tip of Cape Kaneno, which separates {{ill|lt=Genkai Sea|Q498047|WD=Q498047}} and {{ill|lt=Hibikinada|Q11288861|WD=Q11288861}}. A temperate forest has formed within the grounds of Orihata Shrine and on Mt. Sayakata, including more than 10 giant trees of {{ill|lt=Inumaki|Q165063|WD=Q165063}}, {{ill|lt=Chii|Q1065400|WD=Q1065400}}, {{jalink|Hamanabiwa|Q1258941} 4}}・{{ill|lt=Yabunikai|Q161335|WD=Q161335}}・{{ill|lt=Sangoju|Q12600081|WD=Q12600081}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onore|1=UNKNOWN}}・{{ill|lt=Shirodamo|Q5792530|WD=Q5792530}}・{{ill|lt=Tabnoki|Q5792073|WD=Q5792073|lt=Tab}}・{{ill|lt=Tobera|Q161375|WD=Q161375}}・{{ill|lt=Yatsude|Q203569|WD=Q203569}}・{{ill|lt=Tsubaki|Q160121|WD=Q160121}}・{{ill|lt=Yuzuriha|Q846968|WD=Q846968}}・{{ jalink|Yamamomo|Q311617}}・{{ill|lt=Sanekazura|Q1441971|WD=Q1441971}}・{{ill|lt=Fouto Kazura|Q5924111|WD=Q5924111}}・{{ill|lt=Sakaki|Q775470|WD=Q775470}}・{{ill|lt=Aoki (Plants) More than 20 species grow here, including|Q163114|WD=Q163114|lt=Aoki}}, {{ill|lt=Masaki|Q161383|WD=Q161383}}, {{ill|lt=Kijoorchid|Q5367121|WD=Q5367121|lt=Kijoorchid}}, and {{ill|lt=Mukunoki|Q4779482|WD=Q4779482}}. This natural forest has been designated as a natural monument of Fukuoka Prefecture as the ``Orihata Shrine Inumaki Natural Forest'' (Munahaku Electronic Museum). .According to ancient documents, the current main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}8 ({{ill|lt=1695|Q7696|WD=Q7696}}), and the worship hall was built in Genroku 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822}}). Several other companies are also located within the precincts. Also within the precincts are Kutsuzuka, where the enshrined deity, Minister Takeuchi, buried the shoe left after ascending to heaven, {{Sfn|Orihata Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1978}}, and a huge stone related to the legend of the sinking bell, which will be described later. File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-rin.jpg|Precinct forest (Fukuoka Prefecture designated natural monument) File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), Kutsu-zuka.jpg|Kutsuzuka File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-1.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidaisha-2.jpg|Shinuchisha File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), keidai-torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Cultural assets === === Fukuoka Prefecture designated cultural property === * Natural monument ** Inumaki natural forest at Orihata Shrine - Designated on August 13, 1955Explanation board within the precincts. . === Tradition === [[File:Orihata-jinja (Munakata), kyoseki.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Giant stone of the legend of the sunken bell}}]] There is a legend that a foreign bell is sunk in the sea at Cape Kanenomisaki, where Orihata Shrine is located. The place name ``Cape Kaneno'' comes from this, and similar legends of sinking bells are known all over the country, but this area and Kanegasaki in {{ill|lt=Fukui Prefecture|Q133879|WD=Q133879}} are especially famous . However, what was thought to be a bell on the sea floor at Cape Kaneno was brought back to land in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174}}8 ({{ill|lt=1919|Q2157|WD=Q2157}}), but what was pulled up at that time was not a bell but a megalith. This megalith is currently enshrined on the approach to Orihata Shrine. Additionally, the area between Cape Kaneno and {{ill|lt=Jijima|Q17992764|WD=Q17992764}} on the opposite shore is a reef area, and it is known that it has been a key point and difficult point for maritime transportation since ancient times. "{{ill|lt=Manyoushu|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}" Manyoushu volume 7 number 1230 ([http://infux03.inf.edu.yamaguchi-u.ac.jp/~manyou/ver2_2/manyou_kekka2.php?kekka=07/1230 07/1230] reference). So,{{Cquote|Chihayaburu Even if I don't pass the golden cape, I won't forget Shiga's Emperor (Sumekami)|20px||"{{ill|lt=Man'yoshu|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}" Volume 7 No. 1230"New Edition of Japanese Classical Literature Complete Works 7 Man'yoshu (2)” Shogakukan, 2004 ({{ill|lt=Japan Knowledge|Q11309996|WD=Q11309996}} edition), pp. 226-227. }} There is a song about praying to the god of the sea when passing Cape Kaneno (Kin no Misaki) {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. Based on this song, there is a theory that Cape Kanenosaki was once inhabited by the Azum tribe, who worshiped the god Shiga. {{Sfn|Kanasaki/Kanezaki (Heibonsha)|2004}}. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258}}{{ill|lt=Munakata City|Q819603|WD=Q819603}}{{ill|lt=Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|WD=Q11651333|lt=Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 '''Access''' * {{ill|lt=Nishitetsu Bus|Q11629843|WD=Q11629843}}/Munakata City Community Bus get off at "Kyodomari" bus stop (short walk after getting off) - There are only a few buses. haiden.jpg|270px]]
House of worship |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258}}{{ill|lt=Munakata City|Q819603|WD=Q819603}}{{ill|lt=Kanezaki (Munakata City)|Q11651333|WD=Q11651333|lt=Kanezaki}}Aza Misaki 224 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|33|53|16.05|N|130|31|32.05|E|region:JP-39_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Orihata Shrine}} |Enshrined God = {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|lt=Minister Takeuchi}}

Shiga Okami
{{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}}
Munakata Okami
